0.title: Welcome to Stonetop
0.excerpt: Stonetop is a hearth fantasy role-playing game, set in an iron age that never was. It's meant for 3-5 people to play over multiple sessions. Expect each session to last 3-5 hours. Decide which of you will be the game master, or GM for short…

1.title: Expectations
1.excerpt: Stonetop works best with 3-5 people: the GM and 2-4 players. With just one player, some of the mechanics and procedures fall flat. With 5 or more players, it becomes increasingly hard to share the spotlight, make decisions, and keep things…

2.title: Premise
2.excerpt: Everyone should buy in to the game's core premise: PCs are the heroes of an isolated village in a fantasy iron age. They have roots in the village and care about its inhabitants. Adventures involve defending the village from threats or seiz…

3.title: Commitment
3.excerpt: A game of Stonetop requires multiple sessions, each of which will take 3-5 hours. You need at least 3 or 4 sessions to really experience the game, but it's best played long-term, over dozens of sessions. Stonetop is not a good choice for on…

4.title: Structure
4.excerpt: During the first session, you'll create characters together and introduce them to each other. As you do so, you'll create your group's specific version of Stonetop. You'll also roll some dice to find out what the first season of play holds.…

5.title: Dice notation
5.excerpt: If you're unfamiliar: - A "d6" is a six-sided die, a "d10" is a ten-sided die, etc. - "Roll 2d6" means: "Roll two sixsided dice and add the result." - "Roll d10+1" means: "Roll one ten-sided die and add one to the result." The expedition wi…

6.title: Content
6.excerpt: By default, a game of Stonetop features the following content: - Fairly graphic violence - Kidnappings, abductions, families in danger - Bigotry, racial tensions - Horror elements such as mind control and possession - Personal corruption (e…

7.title: Materials
7.excerpt: To play this game face-to-face, you'll need printed materials (playbooks, inserts, and reference sheets), plus pencils to write with. If you play online, you'll need digital versions of the same. You'll also need dice. Stonetop uses the man…

8.title: The setting
8.excerpt: The village of Stonetop ... is home to around 300 folks. It's a nice place, though poor. People look out for each other. ... is built around a massive standing stone of unknown origin, carved with faint runes. Lightning strikes the Stone of…

9.title: Neighbors
9.excerpt: The Forest Folk disappeared ten years ago, and no one knows why. Perhaps the Fae —magical, fickle beings of the Great Wood—had something to do with it? Horrid little crinwin also lurk in the Great Wood. They've grown bolder and more numerou…

10.title: The Makers
10.excerpt: The Makers are long gone, but their ruins (many sized for giants) remain. Stonetop is built on Maker-ruins: an ancient cistern, the crumbling Old Wall, some old foundations. The Ruined Tower is about a day from town. The Great Wood is litte…

11.title: Other things of note
11.breadcrumb: The setting › Other things of note
11.excerpt: The PCs are all human. Strange peoples (like the Forest Folk, the Fae, or the mask-wearing Ustrina from beyond Gordin's Delve) exist but are poorly understood. Wise folk whisper of the Things Below, primeval entities of darkness, corruption…

12.title: The characters
12.excerpt: As a player, you'll pick a playbook for your character. Each represents a local hero, with deep ties to the community. The Blessed is a nature priest who speaks to spirits and beasts. Works subtle magics via sacred markings and materials. T…

13.title: Why play?
13.excerpt: Because the characters of Stonetop are awesome and compelling, capable of epic deeds and yet fallible and human. Because the world of Stonetop is rich and mysterious, familiar and fantastic. You and your friends get to both explore this wor…

15.title: Get ready
15.excerpt: Make sure that everyone is on board with the concept of Stonetop : that they'll play the heroes of a small, isolated village. If someone wants to play a rootless, treasurehunting mercenary, they're unlikely to enjoy themselves, and that wil…

16.title: Review the setting
16.excerpt: Once you've gathered for the first session, ask everyone to take 10-15 minutes to read the Setting Overview handout. Do so even if everyone reviewed the handout in advance. It helps to refresh memories and get everyone on the same page. Whe…

17.title: Set expectations
17.excerpt: Refer to the first page of the Setting Overview. Ask everyone if they're comfortable with the players' agenda and principles . Clarify and answer questions. Emphasize the importance of collaboration. Explain that you have an agenda and prin…

18.title: Discuss tone and content
18.breadcrumb: Getting Started › Discuss tone and content
18.excerpt: Talk to your group about tone : Stonetop takes itself seriously, grounding itself in human concerns. The PCs are exceptional, but they aren't super-human. Violence has consequences. Monsters are scary. Magic is strange and dangerous. People…

19.title: Making corrections
19.excerpt: Mistakes will happen. When excluded content comes up in play, or veiled content gets included on-screen, whoever notices should call "time out" and point out what happened. Don't make a big deal about it; acknowledge the mistake, fix the fi…

20.title: Create characters
20.excerpt: After you review the setting, set expectations, and establish content guidelines, it's time to make characters! Do this together, during the first session. First, have everyone pick a character playbook. Refer to the "Setting Overview" hand…

26.title: Vessel
26.excerpt: - How did you gain or become aware of your power? When did this happen? - How has your life changed since gaining your powers? - Do you have a mentor? Or are you self taught, going by instinct and insight? - Do you hold some formal position…

28.title: The Natural
28.excerpt: - What mundane craft, trade, or skill do you excel at, better than anyone? - What well-earned reputation do you have? - What lies get whispered behind your back? - What secret do you know? About whom?

29.breadcrumb: The Fox › The Natural › A Life of Crime
29.excerpt: - What criminal enterprise were you a part of? Who ran the show? - How did you get into that life? - How did you get out? - Who or what did you leave behind? - Who here knows about your past?

30.breadcrumb: The Fox › The Natural › The Prodigal Returned
30.excerpt: - Why did you leave? How long ago? - Where did you go first? Why? - Where were you the longest? - What brought you home? When? - What has changed most while you were away?

31.title: The Heavy
31.breadcrumb: The Fox › The Natural › The Heavy

32.title: Sheriff
32.excerpt: - Is this a formal position, or just something everyone agrees on? - How long have you acted as sheriff? - Who, if anyone, did this job before you? - Just where exactly did you learn how to fight so well?

33.title: Blood-soaked Past
33.excerpt: - How did you earn your grim reputation? - What did people used to call you, back in your dark bloody days? - What prompted you to put all that behind you? When did that happen? - Who here knows about your past? - Who have you, personally,…

34.title: Storm-marked
34.excerpt: - When did the marks manifest? How? - Are they a symbol of your strength, speed, and courage? Or the source? - Are the marks a blessing? Or a curse? - How do folks in Stonetop generally treat you?

36.title: Legacy

37.title: Missionary
37.excerpt: - Where are you from, originally? Where did you do your apprenticeship? - How were you chosen as an apprentice? - How long were you an apprentice? How long have you been a full Judge? - Of the Judges in the other steadings (Devin, Haeris, e…

38.title: Prophet
38.excerpt: - How did Aratis call you to her service? - What is the symbol of your authority (hammer, shield, or helm)? Where and how did you acquire it? - How did you establish yourself as Stonetop's Judge?

40.title: Auspicious Birth
40.excerpt: - How was your birth marked by Helior? - How did your connection to Helior shape your youth? - Do you hold some formal position as a servant of Helior? - Who in town resents you, or at least what you represent? - Who in town is your most de…

41.title: Itinerant Mystic
41.excerpt: - What is it that keeps drawing you back to Stonetop? - Where else do you regularly travel to? - How long have you lived this wandering lifestyle? - Why is it that some folk from Stonetop are always so happy to see you? - Why is it that som…

42.title: Soul on Fire
42.excerpt: - What sort of vice or iniquity once ruled your life? - What brought you so low, only to find succor in Helior's light? - Do you hold some formal position as a servant of Helior? - How have the people of Stonetop responded to your preaching…

44.title: Scion
44.excerpt: - Who ran the militia before you? - When did you take charge? - Did anyone else want your position? - What is the nature of your crew? Why are they so respected? - How do you recruit or initiate members?

45.title: Penitent

46.title: Luminary
46.excerpt: - Are you touched by the gods? Does ancient blood flow in your veins? Or are you just the one who stepped up when needed? - When and how did it become clear that you were a natural leader? - When did you take charge of the militia? - Who, i…

47.title: The Ranger
47.breadcrumb: Soul on Fire › Penitent › The Ranger

48.title: Mighty Hunter
48.excerpt: - How long have you plied the Wood? Who first taught you their ways? - Did you ever meet the Forest Folk, before they disappeared? Where do you think they went? - What deed are you most renowned for?

50.title: Beast-bonded

52.title: Patriot
52.excerpt: - What grim event convinced you that Stonetop and the people you love are in danger? - Who else knows about your arcana and the powers you tamper with? - How did you learn so much of the Things Below, and your other area of expertise?

53.title: Antiquarian
53.excerpt: - How did you learn so much of the Makers, and your other area of expertise? - What do other scholars claim the Stone really is? What do you think, instead? - What convinced you that Stonetop holds the key to your greatest discoveries? - Wh…

54.title: Witch Hunter
54.excerpt: - What set you on the path of hunting evil? - What have you sacrificed, walking this path? - How did you come to be so well versed in your chosen subjects? - Why do you (still) call Stonetop home?

55.breadcrumb: Wide Wanderer › Patriot › Witch Hunter › The Would-be Hero

56.excerpt: - What was your first taste of danger and excitement? - What do you dream of becoming? - What hero of old do you idolize? - Who have you let down the most?

57.title: Driven
57.breadcrumb: Wide Wanderer › Patriot › Witch Hunter › Driven
57.excerpt: - What happened to set you down this path? - When and where did this happen? - What was your life like before then?

58.title: Destined
58.excerpt: - What are the elements of your destiny? - How and when was your destiny (or what you know of it) revealed to you? - Is your destiny a curse? A blessing? Do you even believe in it? - Who knows about your destiny? How much? How much do they…

59.title: 2 Special possessions
59.excerpt: The next step is to establish each character's special possessions, and how they contribute to the village. During this round, you're trying to figure out where the PCs fit into daily life. Almost everyone in Stonetop helps in the fields, a…

60.title: Smithy or tannery
60.excerpt: - Are you the town's smith/tanner, or their apprentice, or what? - How long have you been doing this? How'd you get started?

61.title: Tools (carpenter's, husbandry, etc.)
61.excerpt: - Do you earn your keep with these tools? Or is it something you do on the side? - Where did you get them? How did you learn to use them?

62.title: Apiary, aviary, chandlery, goat herd, glassworks, or herb garden
62.breadcrumb: 2 Special possessions › Tools (carpenter's, husbandry, etc.) › Apiary, aviary, chandlery, goat herd, glassworks, or herb garden
62.excerpt: - How big is it? And where? - Is it yours? Yours to manage? Or what? - How long has it been in Stonetop? - What's its importance to the village?

63.title: Distillery
63.breadcrumb: 2 Special possessions › Tools (carpenter's, husbandry, etc.) › Distillery
63.excerpt: - Are you the publican? Or related to them? - How long have you been doing this? - How good is your stuff? Whose is better?

64.title: Anything else
64.excerpt: - How did you come to possess that? When? - What do others know/think about it? - What does it mean to you? To the town?

65.title: Finally, ask everyone:
65.breadcrumb: 2 Special possessions › Anything else › Finally, ask everyone:
65.excerpt: Can you read and write? If so, who taught you, and when?

82.excerpt: Ask for details, but leave some questions unanswered. The threat should feel mysterious, at least at the outset. - Where exactly did this happen? When? - Why do you think that?

97.title: The conversation
97.excerpt: You play Stonetop by talking to each other. The GM says something, you and the other players respond. You ask each other questions. You refer to the rules, roll some dice, and talk about what the results mean. You take turns talking, but it…

98.breadcrumb: Playing the Game › The GM is responsible for everything else, including:
98.excerpt: - The world, the terrain, the weather - Monsters and hazards - Non-player characters (NPCs), - What happened before the PCs got here - What happens after they leave The GM will ask for input and ideas. They'll ask you to make up details abo…

99.title: Your agenda
99.excerpt: When you play Stonetop , these should be your goals: - Portray a compelling character - Engage with the fictional world - Play to find out what happens The game's rules and structure assume that you are pursuing these three goals, and no ot…

100.title: The GM's agenda
100.breadcrumb: Playing the Game › The GM's agenda
100.excerpt: In case you're wondering, the GM's agenda is as follows: - Portray a rich, mysterious world - Punctuate the PCs' lives with adventure - Play to find out what happens In other words, they're supposed to present you with a world that's worth…

101.title: Flow of play
101.breadcrumb: Playing the Game › Flow of play
101.excerpt: The GM establishes the situation : where you are, what time it is, who's there, what's going on. They describe the environment, add details, and portray NPCs. They ask questions about what you're doing or what's on your mind. As a player ,…

102.title: Dice and moves
102.breadcrumb: Playing the Game › Dice and moves
102.excerpt: As you all talk about what's happening in the fiction, and what your PCs are doing, you'll often trigger moves . A move is a little bit of rules that shapes the conversation and the fiction. Here's an example:

103.title: CLASH
103.excerpt: When you fight in melee or close quarters , roll +STR: on a 10+ , your maneuver works as expected (deal your damage) and pick 1: - Avoid, prevent, or counter your enemy's attack - Strike hard and fast, for 1d6 extra damage, but suffer your…

104.title: Triggers
104.excerpt: Each move has a trigger , which says when the move kicks in. The trigger is almost always fictional; you say that your character does something that matches the trigger, and the rest of the move tells us how to resolve that action. You migh…

105.title: Rolling
105.excerpt: Many moves tell you to "roll +STAT" (usually with a specific stat, like STR or INT, see Stats). "Roll" means: roll two six-sided dice (2d6), and add them together. "+STAT" means: find that stat in your PC's playbook and add it to the roll.…

106.title: Results
106.excerpt: When a move has you roll, it will also say what happens based on that roll. On a 10+ (meaning, a total roll of 10 or higher), you get a strong hit , the best you can hope for. On a 7-9 (meaning, a total roll of 7, 8, or 9), you get a weak h…

107.title: Hold and spend
107.breadcrumb: Playing the Game › Hold and spend
107.excerpt: Some moves will tell you to "hold x currency," where the currency is something specific like "Preparation" or "Readiness." Note how much you hold, and spend it as described by the move. For example:

108.title: DEFEND
108.excerpt: When you take up a defensive stance or jump in to protect others , roll +CON: on a 10+ , hold 3 Readiness (or 4 if you bear a shield); on a 7-9 , hold 1 Readiness (or 2 with a shield). You can spend Readiness 1-for-1 to: - Suffer an attack'…

109.title: The GM, dice, and moves
109.breadcrumb: Playing the Game › The GM, dice, and moves
109.excerpt: In Stonetop , the GM doesn't normally roll dice and they don't trigger moves. If you're fighting a monster, the GM might declare, "It leaps at you, claws out and hissing." But they don't trigger Clash and they don't roll dice to see if the…

110.title: The Die of Fate
110.breadcrumb: Playing the Game › The Die of Fate
110.excerpt: When the GM wants to let the fates decide an outcome, but there's no move that would apply, they might ask you to roll the Die of Fate. That means: roll a d6 (straight, with no modifiers unless the GM says otherwise). High is good, low is b…

111.title: Fictional positioning
111.excerpt: The actions that your character takes and the moves you trigger are informed by your fictional positioning—the sum of established fictional details about your character and the world around them. This includes (but is not limited to): - You…

112.title: PC vs. PC
112.breadcrumb: Playing the Game › PC vs. PC
112.excerpt: There will be times when you and other PCs come into conflict. You might Persuade another PC to go along with your plan. They might Seek Insight to figure out what you're up to. You might Interfere to keep them from doing something they'll…

113.title: Your principles
113.excerpt: As a player, Stonetop works best if you… - Begin and end with the fiction - Connect with the other PCs - Show us what's important to you - Have goals and pursue them - Be bold, take risks - Embrace difficulty, setback, and failure - Partici…

114.title: Begin and end with the fiction
114.breadcrumb: Your principles › Begin and end with the fiction
114.excerpt: Tell us how you do what you do, what it looks like. Don't just say "I'm Seeking Insight," tell us how . "I cock my head, listening to the sounds of the forest for anything odd." Don't just say "I attack," say what you do . "I rush in, shiel…

115.title: Connect with the other PCs
115.breadcrumb: Your principles › Connect with the other PCs
115.excerpt: Explore the relationships between your character and the other PCs. Play out scenes with each other, speaking in your character's voices. Go out of your way to interact with each other, to care about each other, and to get involved in each…

116.title: Show us what's important to you
116.breadcrumb: Your principles › Show us what's important to you
116.excerpt: Ask yourself, as your character: Who and what will you fight for? What do you prize? What do you aspire to, hope for, dream of? For whom would you lay down your life? Make it clear what you value, through word and deed, and you'll be portra…

117.title: Have goals and pursue them
117.breadcrumb: Your principles › Have goals and pursue them
117.excerpt: Identify things that your PC wants—a steading improvement, an NPC's hand in marriage, the respect of the elders, maybe just a roof that doesn't leak—and actively work towards those things in play. Don't just react to the threats that the GM…

118.title: Be bold, take risks
118.breadcrumb: Your principles › Be bold, take risks
118.excerpt: Take decisive action that moves the game and situation forward. You're the PCs, the folks that Stonetop looks to save them when danger looms. If you don't act like a hero, who will? Avoid being overly cautious, or spending hours on careful,…

119.title: Embrace difficulty, setback, and failure
119.breadcrumb: Your principles › Embrace difficulty, setback, and failure
119.excerpt: Not everything is going to go your way. Part of the GM's job is to present you with adversity and threaten the things you care about. Those challenges are meaningless unless the stakes are real, unless there's a chance you'll lose. Difficul…

120.title: Participate in worldbuilding
120.excerpt: The GM is responsible for the world at large. But as you create your character, your playbook will prompt you to define things about the world: about the god you serve, about the troubles you've seen, about the NPCs who live in Stonetop. Ch…

121.title: Build on what others have said
121.breadcrumb: Your principles › Build on what others have said
121.excerpt: Let yourself be inspired by your fellow players. Take elements that they introduce and use them yourself. Build on them, riff on them, tweak them. Character creation is the most obvious time to build on what others have said. The Fox tells…

122.title: Give others a chance to shine
122.breadcrumb: Your principles › Give others a chance to shine
122.excerpt: Don't hog the spotlight. Be aware of how much you talk, relative to the other players. If you've been talking a lot, suggest a spotlight change. If a quiet player is talking, don't interrupt unless its timely and valuable—and even then, rai…

123.title: Participate in the conversation
123.breadcrumb: Your principles › Participate in the conversation
123.excerpt: You play this game by talking to each other. The game is the conversation. Be an active, engaged part of that conversation. Otherwise, why are you here? Pay attention, even when your PC isn't involved. Try to avoid distractions, in whatever…

124.title: Other things to do
124.breadcrumb: Your principles › Other things to do
124.excerpt: Take notes! NPC names and traits. Who's related to whom. Bits of history and lore. Places. Things. The events of each session. If you think it'll be helpful to reference later, write it down. Your future self will thank you. Learn the rules…

125.title: Advocate for yourself and for each other.
125.breadcrumb: Your principles › Advocate for yourself and for each other.
125.excerpt: If you think the GM has misinterpreted a move, or missed a detail, or skipped someone who's been waiting, say something! If you think another player is being a butt, ask them to knock it off. If you want to see something in the game, make t…

126.title: When in doubt...
126.excerpt: If the GM asks, "What do you do?" and you're unsure, do the following: - Visualize the situation - Ask the GM for clarification - Ask yourself, "What do I want? What's my goal?" - Consider your strengths and weaknesses - Look to others for…

127.title: What not to do
127.breadcrumb: Your principles › What not to do
127.excerpt: Don't roll until there's agreement. If you say "Ooh, I bet I Know Things about Fae" don't just pick up the dice and roll +INT. Wait for the GM and the rest of the group to acknowledge that, yeah, you're triggering a move, this move. If you…

128.title: Your character
128.excerpt: Every PC in Stonetop has a playbook. A playbook is a template, a set of choices and options that point towards a specific character archetype while still leaving room to make the character unique. There are nine standard playbooks , which y…

129.title: Each playbook also includes:
129.breadcrumb: Your character › Each playbook also includes:
129.excerpt: - A place to record debilities - Your damage die - Boxes in which to record your hit points (HP), armor, experience (XP), and current level - A set of prompts and questions that help you define part of the world - A procedure for introducin…

130.title: Background
130.excerpt: Each playbook includes a choice of three backgrounds. Your choice tells us how your PC came to be the exceptional person that they are. It also helps define their backstory, and their role in the village of Stonetop. Each background comes w…

131.title: Instinct
131.excerpt: Your instinct describes how your PC tends to behave, for better or for worse. If you demonstrate your instinct in play, or struggle against it, you get to mark XP at the end of the session . For your first PC, choose one of the instincts li…

132.title: Appearance
132.excerpt: Each playbook prompts you to pick from a number of options for your character's appearance, or to make something else up. This is purely meant to help you visualize and flesh out your PC; it doesn't have any specific mechanical effect. Use…

133.title: Place of origin and name
133.breadcrumb: Your character › Place of origin and name
133.excerpt: Stonetop is home (or close enough) to every PC, but you can decide where your character or your character's family came from originally. If you're not sure what to pick, pick Stonetop. Each playbook presents a different set of possible orig…

134.title: Creating your character
134.excerpt: At the start of a new game , make characters together, during the first session. It's cool to show up with a concept or some ideas in mind, but hold those ideas lightly. Be willing to discard or change them in response to the other players'…

135.title: Stats
135.excerpt: Each PC has six stats. When you trigger a move, and that move involves a roll, the move will tell you which stat to add. Each stat has a three-letter abbreviation, The moves and game text refer almost exclusively to the abbreviation, just f…

136.title: Debilities
136.excerpt: During play, a move or the GM might tell you to "mark a debility." There are three debilities: - Weakened : fatigued, tired, sluggish, shaky. Take disadvantage when rolling +STR or +DEX. - Dazed : out of it, befuddled, not thinking clearly.…

137.title: Hit points (HP)
137.excerpt: Your hit points (HP) represent how much fight you've got left in you. Start play with your current HP equal to your max HP, as noted in your playbook. You lose HP when you take damage—when you are injured, hurt, roughed up, knocked around,…

138.title: XP
138.excerpt: Short for "experience points." You start with no XP. You mark XP… - ... when you roll for a move and get a 6- (unless the move says otherwise); - ... as part of the End of Session move ; and - ... when another move says so . "Mark XP" means…

139.title: Special possessions
139.excerpt: Your special possessions are the unique or interesting things that you own or have access to. In addition to your special possessions, you have access to common weapons, armor, and gear listed on the Inventory insert . See the section on Ge…

140.title: Moves
140.excerpt: Each playbook has a selection of moves. You'll start play with 3 or 4 of them; your playbook tells you which, or which to choose from. Each time you Level Up , you get a new move from your playbook. Some playbook moves lack triggers ; you s…

141.title: World building
141.excerpt: On the back of each playbook, in the left column, you'll find a series of questions and prompts that will help you define the world and your character's place in it. Fill these out on your own, to the best of your ability. At the start of a…

142.title: Introductions
142.excerpt: Each playbook ends with a procedure for introducing your character to the group. This is to meant to happen during the first session, after you all choose and fill out your playbooks. The GM will walk the group through it, together, asking…

143.title: Inserts
143.excerpt: Every playbook comes with an insert, a half-page sheet that you can slip inside your folded playbook. Copies of these are provided for reference in this book, starting on Inserts Every playbook gets an insert for tracking your current inven…

144.title: Arcana
144.excerpt: Arcana are magical items, places, or lore that can provide power—but only if you spend the time and effort to fully unlock their mysteries. The Seeker starts with a collection of arcana, and anyone can acquire them in play. Every arcanum is…

145.title: Minor arcana
145.excerpt: Each minor arcanum is represented by a little card. You can see an example below. The front side, as seen in the example below, shows what you can tell about the arcanum at a glance or with a little study. It also says what's required to un…

146.title: Laoj Davith's Galvanic Infusion
146.breadcrumb: Hit points (HP) › Laoj Davith's Galvanic Infusion
146.excerpt: minor arcanum . When you touch a piece of aetherium and draw its power into yourself , roll +CON: on a 7+ , you absorb the power and gain 1d8 HP (even if this puts you above your max HP); but on a 7-9 , the aetherium is drained and crumbles…

147.title: Major arcana
147.excerpt: Each major arcanum is represented by a half-page insert. They are generally more potent than minor arcana, with the potential to provide multiple moves and powers. But they are also much more dangerous, and using their powers comes at the r…

148.title: Front side
148.excerpt: The front side of each major arcanum shows what you can tell about it at first glance or with a bit of study. Most major arcana provide a move or some other mechanical benefit, even before you unlock their mysteries. The front of a major ar…

149.title: Mysteries
149.excerpt: The back of each major arcanum shows its mysteries: additional moves or options that the arcanum can provide. When you unlock a major arcanum's mysteries, you get access to one or more of these moves or options (the front side will tell you…

150.title: Consequences
150.excerpt: The back of each major arcanum also lists its potential consequences. You mark a consequence when a move tells you to, or when you roll a 6- on a move and the GM tells you to mark one. You get to pick which consequence you mark. Whatever th…

151.title: Noruba's Ice Sphere
151.excerpt: , magical , awkward A perfect sphere of dark ice, the size of a fist, never melting, hard as stone, and set like a gaudy gem at the end of a finely wrought torc. Or not a torc, perhaps, but maybe a ring that once sat upon some giant's finge…

152.breadcrumb: Major arcana › Mysteries of Noruba's Ice Sphere

153.title: Moves
153.breadcrumb: Major arcana › Mysteries of Noruba's Ice Sphere › Moves

154.title: MINDWALKING
154.breadcrumb: Major arcana › Mysteries of Noruba's Ice Sphere › MINDWALKING
154.excerpt: When you use the Ice Sphere as a psychic anchor , your consciousness leaves your body and walks the world as a spirit, invisible and insubstantial. Roll +INT: on a 10+ , hold 3 Power; on a 7-9 , hold 2 Power; on a 6- , hold 2 Power and mark…

156.title: FARWALKER
156.excerpt: When you mindwalk , you may spend 1 Power to instantly send your mind to any place you have ever visited, physically or mentally.

157.title: TELEPATHY
157.excerpt: When you mindwalk , you may spend 1 Power to become able to communicate mentally with someone nearby, as long as you remain in their presence. They can choose whether to respond, and can attempt to ignore you, but cannot shut you out comple…

158.title: TULPA
158.excerpt: When you mindwalk , you may spend 1 Power to animate a mass of loose material (gravel, snow, leaves, etc.) into a body no larger than your own. When you would roll STR, DEX, or CON in this form, use INT instead. This form has 10 HP. When re…

159.title: Consequences
159.excerpt: - Your physical body withers and fades—permanently mark the weakened debility - Your emotions dull. You can no longer be affected by fear, hate, or other powerful emotions (for good or ill). - Replace your instinct with "Detachment: To dist…

160.title: Sharing major arcana
160.excerpt: You can share a major arcanum with another PC or even an NPC, as long as it makes sense to do so. In general: - Each character must unlock the mysteries of the major arcanum on their own. - Each character tracks their own consequences separ…

161.title: Followers
161.excerpt: A follower is a non-player character (NPC) who takes orders from you and generally follows your lead. Followers are NPCs, meaning the GM is ultimately responsible for what they do and say, but… - ... they'll generally do as you say; - ... t…

162.title: Here's how you can get followers:
162.breadcrumb: TULPA › Here's how you can get followers:
162.excerpt: - The Marshal starts with a crew of followers, automatically. Track them on your Crew insert. - The Blessed starts with followers if you take the Initiate background. Track them on the Initiates of Danu insert. - The Ranger starts with a fo…

163.title: Your home
163.excerpt: Every PC lives in or near the village of Stonetop; it's the focal point of play. The village itself has its own playbook, a living document that changes as you play. Stonetop's steading playbook is reproduced starting on The Steading The st…

164.title: Size
164.excerpt: Size reflects the scale of the steading's infrastructure, its baseline population. The possible values for Size are: - hamlet (<50 people) - village (150-350 people) - town (500-1500 people) - city (2500+ people) Size affects how much Surpl…

165.title: Content
165.excerpt: At the beginning of your first session, you and the rest of the group will have a discussion about content. In particular, you'll have a chance to establish: - Excluded content , that won't be part of the game at all (on-camera or off ) - V…

166.title: Steading stats
166.excerpt: Stonetop has four stats, each of which ranges from -1 to +3. The four steading stats are: - Fortunes : morale, social cohesion, and just "how it's going." Roll +Fortunes when Seasons Change to see what happens in the coming months, or when…

167.title: Debilities
167.excerpt: During play, your steading can suffer debilities much like your PC can Debilities. There are three steading debilities: - Diminished , by injury, sickness, doubt. Take disadvantage to Deploy , Muster , or Pull Together . - Lacking , due to…

168.title: Surplus
168.excerpt: Surplus represents food in the granary and in household larders, livestock ready for slaughter, whisky that's ready to drink, fur that's ready to trade or be fashioned into garments. It's a resource that you accumulate, consume, and spend.…

169.title: Resources & fortifications
169.excerpt: Next to the steading's Prosperity, you'll see a list of resources (farming, hunting/ trapping, stone from the Old Wall, etc.). Next to the steading's Defenses, you'll see a list of fortifications and other elements that help keep the villag…

170.title: Places of interest
170.excerpt: The steading playbook includes a map of the village, noting key places like the Stone itself, the granary, the public house, and so forth. As part of introducing your PCs, you'll add their homes to this map. You'll also add any additional p…

171.title: Steading improvements
171.excerpt: The steading playbook includes a number of potential improvements that you can pursue. There are also spots for new improvements, which the GM might reveal to you in play. Each steading improvement has a list of requirements, which you can…

172.title: Assets
172.excerpt: After the improvements, the steading playbook has a section for recording assets that are owned by the residents of Stonetop in common: a pair of draft horses, a couple iron plows, a couple carts, a horse-drawn wagon, and anything else the…

173.title: NPCs
173.excerpt: Finally, the steading playbook has places to record notable non-player characters (NPCs). There's a section for Residents of Stonetop , and one for Notable neighbors of Stonetop . Each time you establish a new NPC (during character introduc…

174.title: Other ways to track NPCs
174.breadcrumb: TULPA › Other ways to track NPCs
174.excerpt: A game of Stonetop can easily involve dozens of NPCs, and space on the steading playbook is limited. Your group might opt to use a digital tool for tracking NPCs, such as: - A relationship map, to visually organize PCs, NPCs, and their rela…

175.title: The moves
175.excerpt: The following moves are available to all PCs. They're organized based on the context in which they apply. Remember, if a move calls for a roll and doesn't say what happens on a 6-, mark XP and prepare for the worst .

176.title: Basic moves
176.excerpt: These are the most commonly triggered moves, the ones that can come up in many different contexts.

177.title: AID
177.excerpt: When you help someone who has not yet rolled , the GM picks 1: - They can accomplish more than they could alone - They gain advantage on their roll. Either way, you are exposed to any risk, cost, or consequence associated with their roll. F…

178.title: CLASH
178.excerpt: When you fight in melee or close quarters , roll +STR: on a 10+ , your maneuver works as expected (deal your damage) and pick 1. - Avoid, prevent, or counter your enemy's attack - Strike hard and fast, for 1d6 extra damage, but suffer your…

179.title: DEFEND
179.excerpt: When you take up a defensive stance or jump in to protect others , roll +CON: on a 10+ , hold 3 Readiness (or 4 if you bear a shield); on a 7-9 , hold 1 Readiness (or 2 with a shield). You can spend Readiness 1-for-1 to: - Suffer an attack'…

180.title: DEFY DANGER
180.excerpt: When danger looms, the stakes are high, and you do something chancy , check if another move applies. If not, roll... - ... +STR to power through or test your might - ... +DEX to employ speed, agility, or finesse - ... +CON to endure or hold…

181.title: INTERFERE
181.excerpt: When you try to foil another PC's action and neither of you back down, roll... - ... +STR to power through or test your might - ... +DEX to employ speed, agility, or finesse - ... +CON to endure or hold steady - ... +INT to apply expertise…

182.title: KNOW THINGS
182.excerpt: When you consult your accumulated knowledge , roll +INT: on a 10+ , the GM will tell you something interesting and useful about the topic at hand; on a 7-9 , the GM will tell you something interesting—it's on you to make it useful; either w…

183.title: LET FLY
183.excerpt: When you take an easy shot with a ranged weapon , deal your damage. If the shot is tricky or you're under pressure , first roll +DEX: on a 10+ , you have a clear shot, deal your damage; on a 7-9 , pick 1: - Deal your damage, but deplete you…

184.title: PERSUADE (vs. NPCs)
184.excerpt: When you press or entice an NPC , say what you want them to do (or not do). If they have reason to resist , roll +CHA: on a 10+ , they either do as you want or reveal the easiest way to convince them; on a 7-9 , they reveal something you ca…

185.title: PERSUADE (vs. PCs)
185.excerpt: When you press or entice a PC and they resist , ask their player: "Could I possibly get you to do this, yes or no?" If they say "no," let it drop. If they say "yes," you can roll +CHA: on a 10+ , they mark XP if they do what you want, and i…

186.title: SEEK INSIGHT
186.excerpt: When you study a situation or person , looking to the GM for insight, roll +WIS: on a 10+ , ask the GM 3 questions from the list below; on a 7-9 , ask 1; either way , take advantage on your next move to act on the answers. - What happened h…

187.title: Special moves
187.excerpt: These moves are always in play, but they either affect other moves or are triggered by very specific circumstances.

188.title: ADVANTAGE/DISADVANTAGE
188.excerpt: When you make a roll with advantage , roll an extra die and discard the lowest result. When you make a roll with disadvantage , roll an extra die and discard the highest result. When you make a roll with both advantage and disadvantage , th…

189.title: BURN BRIGHTLY
189.excerpt: When you have enough XP to Level Up (6 + twice your current level), you may spend 2 XP after any roll to add +1 to that roll (maximum +1 per roll). For more information, see Burn Brightly.

190.title: END OF SESSION
190.excerpt: When a session ends , point out how you demonstrated or struggled with your instinct. If you can, mark XP. Say how your relationship with or opinion of a PC, NPC, or group has changed. If you can, mark XP. Answer these questions as a group.…

191.title: Follower moves
191.excerpt: These moves apply when you interact with your followers .

192.title: ORDER FOLLOWERS
192.excerpt: When you direct your follower to do something that would trigger a player move , and they do it, they trigger the move. If the move involves rolling, you roll for them. Instead of rolling +STAT, roll and… - … if they have at least one appro…

193.title: STRENGTHEN YOUR BOND
193.excerpt: When you pay your follower's cost , and you haven't done so recently, they hold +1 Loyalty (max 3). Spend your follower's Loyalty 1-for-1 to have them: - Overcome their fear to do as you say - Resist acting on their instinct/ tags/traits -…

194.title: Expedition moves
194.excerpt: These moves apply when you prepare for, undertake, or return from an expedition.

195.title: CHART A COURSE
195.excerpt: When you wish to travel to a distant place , name or describe your destination ("Gordin's Delve," "the hagr's lair," or "wherever these tracks lead"). If the route is unclear, tell the GM how you intend to reach it. The GM will then tell yo…

196.title: OUTFIT
196.excerpt: When you prepare for an expedition in a friendly community , mark as many on your Inventory insert as you wish to carry, either on specific items or in "undefined." Mark up to 3 for a light load (quick and quiet), 4-6 for a normal load, or…

197.title: REQUISITION
197.excerpt: When you borrow some of the steading's assets for an expedition (like the horses or a plow), roll +Fortunes: on a 10+, go ahead, but bring it back safely; on a 7-9, you'll need to do some convincing; on a 6-, don't mark XP—you can take the…

198.title: HAVE WHAT YOU NEED
198.breadcrumb: Expedition moves › HAVE WHAT YOU NEED
198.excerpt: When you decide that you had something all along , transfer a mark (or marks) from your "undefined" inventory to a specific item or a slot. If you mark a slot, fill it with a common mundane item or something from your personal possessions.…

199.title: RECOVER
199.breadcrumb: Expedition moves › HAVE WHAT YOU NEED › RECOVER
199.excerpt: When you take time to catch your breath and tend to what ails you , expend 1 use of supplies and recover HP equal to 4+Prosperity. You can't gain this benefit again until you take more damage. When you tend to a debility or a problematic wo…

200.title: STRUGGLE AS ONE
200.excerpt: When you Defy Danger as a group , establish the party's approach and each roll +STAT (per Defy Danger): on a 6- , you find yourself in a spot, the GM will describe it or ask you to; on a 7-9 , you pull your weight; on a 10+ , you do well en…

201.title: KEEP COMPANY
201.breadcrumb: Expedition moves › STRUGGLE AS ONE › KEEP COMPANY
201.excerpt: When you spend a stretch of time together, ask the others if they want to Keep Company. If they do, take turns asking a PC or NPC one of the following. - What do you do that's annoying/ endearing? - What do I do that you find annoying/endea…

202.title: MAKE CAMP
202.excerpt: When you settle in to rest in an unsafe area , answer the GM's questions about your campsite. Each member of the party must consume 1 use of supplies or provisions; if you use a mess kit ( requires fire & water ), then 1 use can provide for…

203.title: FORAGE
203.excerpt: When you spend a few hours seeking food in the wild , roll +WIS. In winter, you have disadvantage. On a 10+ , pick 2; on a 7-9 , pick 1: - You acquire <sup 4</sup provisions (1d6 uses) - You acquire an extra 1d6 uses of provisions - You dis…

204.title: Homefront moves
204.excerpt: These moves apply during downtime, or when you're interacting with a steading's population and resources.

205.title: BOLSTER
205.excerpt: When you prepare for what's coming or seek the favor of the gods , say how and answer the GM's questions. Then, hold Preparation based on the amount of time you devote: - A week or so: 1 Preparation - A month or so: 2 Preparation - The bett…

206.title: CONVALESCE
206.excerpt: When you rest for a few days in safety and comfort, set your HP back to your max and clear all your debilities. When you rest for a few weeks under the care of a healer , you heal any problematic wounds that can heal. If you have suffered a…

207.title: DEPLOY
207.excerpt: When you send a steading's people into danger or rally them against an attack , roll +Defenses: on a 10+ , it goes as well as can be expected; on a 7-9 , it works but someone chooses 1 from the list below. If the steading is acting from a p…

208.title: LEVEL UP
208.excerpt: When you have a quiet stretch of time at home and XP equal to (or greater than) 6 + twice your current level , follow these steps: - 1) Subtract 6 + twice your current level from your XP. - 2) Increase your level by 1. - 3) Choose a new mov…

209.title: MAKE A PLAN
209.excerpt: When you wish to accomplish some project but aren't sure how to go about it , tell the GM what you hope to achieve. They'll say what's required. If you're stumped on how to accomplish one of the requirements, tell the GM and Make a Plan for…

210.title: MEET WITH DISASTER
210.excerpt: When calamity befalls the steading or panic spreads , reduce Fortunes by 1 (min -1). If Fortunes would drop below -1 for any reason (not just calamity or panic), then the GM picks 1 instead: - The steading marks diminished from injuries/sic…

211.title: MUSTER
211.excerpt: When you press every able body into the defense of a steading , reduce Fortunes by 1 and roll +Population: on a 7+ , the steading is alert and ready for action until the threat passes, the Seasons Change, or you cease to oversee the muster.…

212.title: PULL TOGETHER
212.excerpt: When you set a community to work on improvements, to secure new resources, or to make major repairs , spend whatever the GM says is required (time, material, Surplus, etc.) and roll +Population: on a 10+ , the job gets done; on a 7-9 , pick…

213.title: TRADE & BARTER
213.excerpt: When you wish to acquire or sell a commonly available item , you can. When you seek to acquire or sell a special item , roll +Prosperity and subtract the item's Value. In winter, you have disadvantage. On a 10+ , you can get it or sell it f…

214.title: SEASONS CHANGE
214.excerpt: When spring bursts forth upon the land , whoever is the most hopeful rolls +Fortunes: on a 10+ , pick 1 seasonal gain; on a 7-9 , pick 1 seasonal gain, but a threat to the steading makes itself known or gets worse; on a 6- , threats abound…

215.title: Seasonal gainsSeasonal gain
215.excerpt: - Population boom: A number of youth come of age, and/or outsiders settle here. Increase Population by 1 (max +3). - Tor's blessing: Fine weather abounds. Take +1 to Pull Together this season, and any time you roll the Die of Fate for weath…

216.title: Gear and possessions
216.excerpt: You don't keep track of every item or possession that your PC owns. You and your family have a whole house full of belongings, and you're part of a tight-knit community that freely shares, gifts, and trades goods with each other. In general…

217.title: Inventory
217.excerpt: When you Outfit for an expedition Outfit, you'll fill out your Inventory insert. Mark the $\diamondsuit$ , $\diamondsuit\diamondsuit$ , or $\square$ next to an item to indicate you are carrying it. Every PC has the same Inventory insert. It…

218.title: Load
218.excerpt: Items that have a $\diamondsuit$ or $\diamondsuit\diamondsuit$ in front of them contribute to your load. Count the total number of $\diamondsuit$ that you have marked. If you have... - ... up to 3 �, then you have a light load , able to mov…

219.title: Small items
219.excerpt: A small item is something that could fit in a pocket, pouch, or boot with room to spare. Small items do not contribute to your load. Track them on the right side of your Inventory insert, in the "Small items" section. When you Outfit , you…

220.title: Undefined items
220.excerpt: You don't have to define your items up front. When you Outfit , you can put some or all of your marks in the "Undefined" areas of your Inventory insert. These represent items that your PC has on their person, but which you (the player) have…

221.title: Supplies
221.excerpt: Near the top of your Inventory insert, you'll find " Supplies" (and " More supplies" and " Even more supplies"). "Supplies" represent the essentials that you might bring with you on expedition: food, water, bandages, salves, whisky, additio…

222.title: Provisions
222.excerpt: If you're low on supplies, you can Forage and acquire up to 2d6 uses of <sup 4</sup provisions. You might also gain them incidentally (if, say, you fight an angry boar and harvest the meat). Expend provisions in place of supplies when you M…

223.title: Back home
223.excerpt: When you return home, clear the marks from your Inventory insert; it rarely matters what you're carrying while you're in town. If it does matter, assume that you have a light load (up to 3 4) and a handful of small items unless there's a go…

224.title: Special possessions
224.excerpt: Your special possessions are the things that you own or have access to, but which others do not . A shield, spear, a cloak, a pair of snow shoes, a cookpot—every household has these, so they are common items. But few (if any) folks in town…

225.title: Initial special possessions
225.excerpt: When you make your character, you'll choose special possessions from your playbook. Most of these choices include a list of items, which you own or have ready access to. Assume that you're proficient with these items. Items listed in the pl…

226.title: Possessions gained in play
226.breadcrumb: Expedition moves › Possessions gained in play
226.excerpt: When you acquire a new item in play, add it to your Inventory insert in the appropriate spot. When you return home, leave it on your insert. It's now one of your special possessions; you can mark it when you Outfit or Have What You Need. Yo…

227.title: Trade & Barter
227.excerpt: Commonly available items—things on the Inventory insert, or that could be found in pretty much any Stonetop household—are readily available. Folks trade, loan, and gift these items all the time, and its not worth tracking them. See Playing…

228.title: Value
228.excerpt: The world of Stonetop has no central government, no common currency, and no standard exchange rates. So, what constitutes a fair trade? To help answer that question, items and services are given a Value from 0 to 4. Value is more of a logar…

229.title: A Value 0 item is generally worth:
229.breadcrumb: Expedition moves › A Value 0 item is generally worth:
229.excerpt: - A purse of copper coins - A single silver coin - A favor - A few days of unskilled labor - A common, mundane item

230.title: A Value 1 item is generally worth:
230.breadcrumb: Expedition moves › A Value 1 item is generally worth:
230.excerpt: - A handful of silver coins - A season (or so) of unskilled labor - A few days of skilled labor - A unit of trade goods\ (sack of grain, pouch of salt, stack of pelts, etc.) - A bit of finery ( richly embroidered cloak, a silk scarf, silver…

231.title: A Value 2 item is generally worth:
231.breadcrumb: Expedition moves › A Value 2 item is generally worth:
231.excerpt: - A purse of silver coins - A Surplus - A single gold coin - A year (or so) of unskilled labor - A season (or so) of skilled labor - A cartload of trade goods\ - An item of luxury or status (gold ring, artful silver torc, gemstone, etc.)

232.title: A Value 3 item is generally worth:
232.breadcrumb: Expedition moves › A Value 3 item is generally worth:
232.excerpt: - A handful of gold coins - A year (or so) of skilled labor - A good, trained horse or mule - A precious item (ruby ring, gold torc, etc.)

233.title: A Value 4 item is generally worth:
233.breadcrumb: Expedition moves › A Value 4 item is generally worth:
233.excerpt: - A purse of gold coins - A dozen or so good horses - A "priceless" item (huge flawless gemstone, gold statuette, bejeweled scepter, etc.) - \ Exotic trade goods (like silks, spices, and the like) are +1 Value Commonly available items are V…

234.title: Coins
234.excerpt: Stonetop doesn't mint coins, and folks don't use them day-to-day. The local economy runs on trade, gifts and favors, debt and honor. Coin is used almost exclusively when trading with outsiders. Even then, coin is worth only what folks will…

235.title: Gear terms & tags
235.excerpt: Many items appear with a list of terms after their name, in parentheses. Terms in italic typeface are tags . They have no specific mechanical effect, but they are part of fictional positioning . The most common tags are listed here, but oth…

256.title: If you want to…
256.breadcrumb: Expedition moves › If you want to…
256.excerpt: Here are some things you might want to do, and how you might go about them.

257.title: … increase Fortunes
257.excerpt: The main ways are: - Undertake an expedition on behalf of the village and Return Triumphant . - Complete a steading improvement that increases Fortunes.

258.title: … gain Surplus
258.excerpt: The village generates Surplus each summer and at the end of autumn when the harvest is complete. If you want more: - Choose "unexpected bounty" when you get a seasonal gain . - Choose "trade opportunity" when you get a seasonal gain, and su…

259.title: …improve Defenses
259.excerpt: You can temporarily boost Defenses via the Muster move Meet with Disaster, but it will usually cost you Fortunes to do so. Other options include: - Build a Palisade or a Stone Wall . They grant advantage to Deploy rolls when you take advant…

260.title: … increase Population
260.excerpt: Choose "population boom" when you get a seasonal gain . Alternatively, go out and save or recruit or capture a bunch of people and bring them back to live in your village. Careful: higher Population makes it easier to Muster and Pull Togeth…

261.title: … improve Prosperity
261.excerpt: Pursue the Expanded Trades and/or the Market improvement. Each of these increase Prosperity by 1, as long as they're maintained. Also, the Township improvement Township grants advantage when you Trade & Barter .

262.title: … unlock a steading improvement
262.breadcrumb: Expedition moves › … unlock a steading improvement
262.excerpt: Look at the requirements and choose one to pursue. If you don't know how to pursue it, Know Things and/or Make a Plan . If you want to make an improvement to the steading that isn't listed, tell the GM what you have in mind. They'll either…

263.title: … get some coin
263.excerpt: You can Outfit or Have What You Need to produce a handful of coppers, but if you want more significant quantities, or any quantity of silver or gold coins, then you'll need to roll Trade & Barter to see if they're even available. Even if th…

264.title: Other options include:
264.excerpt: - If you need more coin than what's available in town, you can undertake a trade mission. Take some of the steading's Surplus to Marshedge, Gordin's Delve, or maybe the Hillfolk, and find someone to buy it. A purse or two of silvers is a fa…

265.title: … find new arcana
265.excerpt: Know Things ! Maybe you know where something like that might be found. If you don't, try something else: - Ask around, dig through the Chronicle (if there is one), consult the spirits (if you can), pray for guidance (if that's your thing),…

266.title: … unlock the mysteries of an arcanum
266.breadcrumb: … unlock the mysteries of an arcanum
266.excerpt: Do what the requirements say! If you're not sure how to meet a requirement, roll to Know Things and/or talk to the GM and Make a Plan .

267.title: … recruit followers
267.breadcrumb: … unlock the mysteries of an arcanum › … recruit followers
267.excerpt: If you have someone in mind, ask them to join you on an expedition. Persuade them if necessary Seek Insight. If you don't have someone in mind, tell the GM what sort of follower you're looking for ("a skilled tracker," "a knowledgeable guid…

268.title: Alternatively:
268.breadcrumb: … unlock the mysteries of an arcanum › … recruit followers › Alternatively:
268.excerpt: - Muster and pick "1 or 2 individuals show real potential." Let the GM know that you're hoping for a follower, and they'll probably work with you. - Go to a bigger town and find a public place, like the Swap in Gordin's Delve or Edgemarket…

269.title: Playbooks & Inserts
269.excerpt: [!note] Restructured chapter The playbooks, inserts, and steading playbook each live in their own note in the Playbooks folder, with the 2nd-printing PDF embedded and the extracted text (errata applied) below it. This note keeps the chapter…

270.title: This chapter is addressed to players, but GMs should read it, too!
270.breadcrumb: Playbooks & Inserts › This chapter is addressed to players, but GMs should read it, too!
270.excerpt: The contents of this chapter are available as PDFs in a downloadable playkit at www.lampblackandbrimstone.com. They are included here for reference, not for use during play.

271.title: Playbooks
271.excerpt: When you create a PC for Stonetop , the first thing you'll do is pick your playbook. Each playbook contains a set of choices, prompts, and options that point towards a specific character archetype, while leaving room to make your character…

272.title: The GM has a playbook, too
272.breadcrumb: Playbooks & Inserts › The GM has a playbook, too
272.excerpt: When you download the playkit from lampblackandbrimstone.com, you'll also find a PDF of the GM playbook, a collection of rules, maps, and tools for the GM to quickly reference during play and prep. It's a summary of content found elsewhere…

273.title: Running the Game
273.excerpt: Your agenda The core loop The spotlight Scenes and loose play Your GM moves Example of play Your principles Other things to do GMing is a practice Prep There are dozens of ways to GM a roleplaying game. There's a particular way to run Stone…

274.title: Your agenda
274.excerpt: These are your goals when running Stonetop : - Portray a rich and mysterious world - Punctuate the characters' lives with adventure - Play to find out what happens Everything you say and do as the GM is meant to support these three goals, a…

275.title: The core loop
275.excerpt: When you run this game, this is what you do . There's more to it, of course, but this is the underlying structure of the conversation between you and the players.

276.title: 1) Establish the situation
276.breadcrumb: The core loop › 1) Establish the situation
276.excerpt: - Frame the action - Describe the environment - Give details and specifics - Ask questions, ask for input - Portray NPCs and monsters Answer questions, clarify

277.title: 2) Make a soft GM move
277.breadcrumb: The core loop › 2) Make a soft GM move

278.title: 3) Ask "What do you do?"
278.breadcrumb: The core loop › 2) Make a soft GM move › 3) Ask "What do you do?"

279.title: 4) Resolve their actions
279.breadcrumb: The core loop › 4) Resolve their actions
279.excerpt: - If they trigger a player move, do what the move says. - If they roll a 6- or they ignore trouble, make a hard GM move (establish badness). - Otherwise, say what happens.

280.title: 5) Repeat
280.breadcrumb: The core loop › 4) Resolve their actions › 5) Repeat
280.excerpt: - Is the situation clear and compelling? Can the PCs act? Back to step 3. - Is the situation clear, but escalating before the PCs act? Back to step 2. - Is the situation clear, but needs a nudge? Back to step 2. - Is the situation unclear?…

281.title: 1 Establish the situation
281.excerpt: It's your job to establish the situations that the PCs find themselves in. Do any or all of the following, as needed, in whatever order makes sense.

282.title: Frame the action
282.breadcrumb: 1 Establish the situation › Frame the action
282.excerpt: Determine who, where, and when . Address one or more of the PCs and say (or ask) where they are. Say (or ask) who else is there. Say (or ask) when this is happening (the season, the time of day, how long after the last scene or situation).

283.title: Describe the environment
283.breadcrumb: 1 Establish the situation › Describe the environment
283.excerpt: For a place you haven't seen "on camera" before, say (or ask) what it's like. For a place that you've seen before, say (or ask) how it's changed (if at all). Include up to 3 strong impressions from different senses—the shocking red of blood…

284.title: Give details and specifics
284.breadcrumb: 1 Establish the situation › Give details and specifics
284.excerpt: Say (or ask) what's happening. Who's doing what? Where is everyone relative to each other? What can the characters see, hear, and sense? Again, focus on what matters. In a social situation, focus on people: who's there, how they look, how t…

285.title: Improvisation & prep
285.excerpt: Yes, you're making all this stuff up (or asking the players to). You'll do some prep in advance (see Prep) or pull from the setting guide, but during play you improvise many of the specifics. Once a detail is established, treat it like it's…

286.title: Ask questions
286.excerpt: You don't have to provide all the details yourself. One of your principles Your principles is to ask questions and build on the answers . Invite the players to contribute to the scene by asking their characters about stuff that they know, f…

287.title: Portray NPCs and monsters
287.breadcrumb: 1 Establish the situation › Portray NPCs and monsters
287.excerpt: If there are non-player characters (NPCs) involved, describe them. Say what they're doing. If the PCs interact with them, say how the NPCs respond. Speak in their voice and say what they say. If that's daunting for you, or you just want to…

288.title: Answer questions, clarify
288.breadcrumb: 1 Establish the situation › Answer questions, clarify
288.excerpt: The players will interrupt you and ask questions. Be honest and generous with truth and detail. If the answer wouldn't be clear to the PCs, say so and tell them what it'd take to figure that out. Your job is to portray a rich and mysterious…

289.title: 2 Make a soft GM move
289.breadcrumb: 1 Establish the situation › 2 Make a soft GM move
289.excerpt: The situation is clear? Good. Now, make a soft GM move—say something to provoke action and/or increase tension. Pick one of your GM moves and do that. To provoke action: say that something bad is about to happen, or is in the process of hap…

290.title: 3 "What do you do?"
290.breadcrumb: 1 Establish the situation › 3 "What do you do?"
290.excerpt: After you make a soft GM move, ask "What do you do?" This hands the conversation back to the player(s) and prompts them to say something. You don't have to literally say, "What do you do?" Sometimes, your move will include a question. When…

291.title: Players jumping in
291.breadcrumb: 1 Establish the situation › Players jumping in
291.excerpt: Players might seize the initiative and declare an action before you ask "What do you do?" or even before you've made a soft GM move. Great! It means they're engaged, and the situation is compelling enough that they want to act on it. You ca…

292.title: 4 Resolve their action
292.breadcrumb: 1 Establish the situation › 4 Resolve their action
292.excerpt: The player says what their character does. Establish how the situation changes, and/or what their action reveals about the situation. Did they trigger a player move? If so, which one? Resolve that move. If you aren't sure whether they trigg…

293.title: Hard GM moves
293.breadcrumb: 1 Establish the situation › Hard GM moves
293.excerpt: Make a hard GM move when a PC... - ... ignores a threat or trouble entirely; or - ... triggers a move but rolls a 6- (and the move doesn't say otherwise). Making a hard GM move means that you establish badness . Describe the consequences of…

294.title: The spotlight
294.excerpt: As you play the game, you'll change who you're addressing, moment to moment. Whoever you're talking to right now is in the spotlight . Sometimes you'll keep the spotlight unfocused and address the group as a whole. Often, you'll focus it on…

295.title: Split the party
295.breadcrumb: 1 Establish the situation › Split the party
295.excerpt: It's going to happen. The PCs will split up—intentionally or because you separate them or capture someone —and now you've got two smaller groups. It's okay. Splitting the party can be a lot of fun. Splitting the party means that you're jugg…

296.title: Scenes and loose play
296.breadcrumb: 1 Establish the situation › Scenes and loose play
296.excerpt: During play, the conversation will naturally shift between scenes and loose play. This isn't a formal distinction, or even always a conscious one. You'll often shift between them naturally and instinctively. Scenes are zoomed-in . They feat…

297.title: Your GM moves
297.breadcrumb: 1 Establish the situation › Your GM moves
297.excerpt: When it's time to make a GM move and you're not sure what to say, pick one of these and do it. These are your basic GM moves : - <sup ä</sup Announce trouble (future or offscreen) - <sup ä</sup Reveal an unwelcome truth - <sup ä</sup Ask a…

298.title: Making Your Move
298.breadcrumb: 1 Establish the situation › Making Your Move
298.excerpt: Whenever the situation is clear and you want to push the action along , make a soft move. Say something that prompts the PCs to act and/or increases the tension. Make soft moves all the time, as part of the core loop of the game. When the P…

299.title: Along the same lines, your moves should reflect the specifics of the situation.
299.breadcrumb: 1 Establish the situation › Along the same lines, your moves should reflect the specifics of the situation.
299.excerpt: Say that a PC faces a charging aurochs, triggers Clash, and rolls a 6-. If the PC is a mighty-thewed Heavy who braced his spear against the charge, then your hard move would look very different than if the PC was a willowy Fox who tried to…

300.title: Other GM moves
300.breadcrumb: 1 Establish the situation › Other GM moves
300.excerpt: You have other lists of GM moves available to you, too. Use your exploration moves when the PCs are on an expedition or exploring a site (see GM exploration moves). Use threat moves to bring a threat or its influence into a scene, or when i…

302.title: <sup>ä</sup> Announce trouble (future or offscreen)
302.breadcrumb: Examples of GM moves › <sup>ä</sup> Announce trouble (future or offscreen)
302.excerpt: This is one of your most versatile moves. Trouble almost always provokes a reaction, or at least gets folks worrying and cranks up the tension. As a hard move, you can turn a previously peaceful scene into a fight or conflict, or maybe make…

303.title: "WHOOMP WHOOMP WHOOMP!
303.breadcrumb: Examples of GM moves › "WHOOMP WHOOMP WHOOMP!
303.excerpt: Rhianna, there's a banging at your door, waking you up from that much-needed sleep. Lowri's calling your name and you hear the watch-bells clanging. What do you do?" "As Caradoc and Blodwen leave, this nasty piece of work watches them go. H…

304.title: <sup>ä</sup> Reveal an unwelcome truth
304.breadcrumb: Examples of GM moves › "WHOOMP WHOOMP WHOOMP! › <sup>ä</sup> Reveal an unwelcome truth
304.excerpt: Establish something as true, something that they really wish wasn't. Maybe it was true all along but you're just revealing it now. Or maybe (especially as a hard move) you're deciding right now that this bad thing is true and letting them k…

305.title: <sup>ä</sup> Ask a provocative question
305.breadcrumb: Examples of GM moves › <sup>ä</sup> Ask a provocative question
305.excerpt: Ask a question that spurs a decision or response, even if it's just an emotional response or a decision not to act. You can ask the question yourself or put it in the mouth of one of your NPCs. Alternately: ask a question that asserts badne…

306.title: <sup>ä</sup> Put someone in a spot
306.breadcrumb: Examples of GM moves › <sup>ä</sup> Put someone in a spot
306.excerpt: This is a more aggressive version of announcing trouble —they've got to do something, now, or it'll get ugly. "Someone" can be a PC or an NPC, and you can play with the spotlight. Maybe the person you put in the spot isn't the one that does…

307.title: <sup>ä</sup> Use up their resources
307.breadcrumb: Examples of GM moves › <sup>ä</sup> Use up their resources
307.excerpt: Something happens that whittles away at their stuff, or that takes it away from them entirely. "Resources" here can be... - ... concrete and specific (their shield, their torch, their lamp oil); - ... concrete but abstract (supplies, ammo);…

308.title: <sup>ä</sup> Demonstrate a downside
308.breadcrumb: Examples of GM moves › <sup>ä</sup> Demonstrate a downside
308.excerpt: Show the limits of their moves, or the baggage that comes with being (e.g.) the Heavy. Show them just how a crude weapon fares against an iron sword or shield. Show them why wearing warm armor in the blazing sun is a bad idea. Show them why…

309.title: <sup>ä</sup> Turn their move back on them
309.breadcrumb: Examples of GM moves › <sup>ä</sup> Turn their move back on them
309.excerpt: This is a good one to use on a 6-. Take the move they were making, and flip it somehow. Maybe you give the advantage to their foes, maybe you have them come up with something bad for you to work with, maybe their move constrains their optio…

310.title: <sup>ä</sup> Hurt someone
310.breadcrumb: Examples of GM moves › <sup>ä</sup> Hurt someone
310.excerpt: Inflict a specific, problematic injury or wound on a PC or an NPC they care about. Something more than just losing HP. Something painful, bloody, consequential, or all three. Using this move definitely means the victim takes damage, and aga…

311.title: Dealing damage and inflicting debilities
311.breadcrumb: Examples of GM moves › Dealing damage and inflicting debilities
311.excerpt: "Dealing damage" isn't a GM move by itself; it's a thing that happens as part of another GM move. When you make a move that involves a character getting banged up, hurt, knocked around, or injured, then deal damage to them as part of that m…

312.title: <sup>ä</sup> Separate them
312.breadcrumb: Examples of GM moves › <sup>ä</sup> Separate them
312.excerpt: Split the party. Put foes or obstacles between them. Have someone fall behind. Have a follower or other NPC go missing. Send someone tumbling down the slope, taking them out of the fight. That sort of thing. This move often pairs well with…

313.title: <sup>ä</sup> Capture someone
313.breadcrumb: Examples of GM moves › <sup>ä</sup> Capture someone
313.excerpt: On a soft version of this move, capture them but stay in the scene, giving them a chance to escape (or others a chance to rescue them). On a hard version, maybe cut straight to the next scene—captured, bound, dragged off who knows where—and…

314.title: <sup>ä</sup> Offer an opportunity (with or without a cost)
314.breadcrumb: Examples of GM moves › <sup>ä</sup> Offer an opportunity (with or without a cost)
314.excerpt: Give them an opening, a chance to act. The opportunity can be specific ("If you act now, you'll have the drop on them.") or general ("They don't seem to have spotted you, what do you do?"). It can come with a definite cost ("...but it'll ma…

315.title: <sup>ä</sup> Tell them the consequences/ requirements (then ask)
315.breadcrumb: Examples of GM moves › <sup>ä</sup> Tell them the consequences/ requirements (then ask)
315.excerpt: Use this move as an interrupt, as a way to clarify the fiction and set the stakes when they say they want to do a thing. "If you do that, you realize that \ \ , right?" Use this move, also, to give them a path forward when the way is otherw…

316.title: <sup>ä</sup> Advance towards impending doom
316.breadcrumb: Examples of GM moves › <sup>ä</sup> Advance towards impending doom
316.excerpt: Threats and hazards might have impending dooms, each with a series of grim portents leading up to them. When you make this move, you decide that one of those grim portents has come to pass, and show it to the characters. In practice, this m…

317.title: Example of play
317.breadcrumb: Examples of GM moves › Example of play
317.excerpt: Everyone's arrived. We've chatted, gotten comfortable, and we're ready to get started. The session starts in loose play. I ask if anyone wants to recap. Rhianna's player does. "We sent a logging crew to the Foothills. But some sort of goat-…

318.title: Your principles
318.breadcrumb: Examples of GM moves › Your principles
318.excerpt: These principles are your guidelines, your best practices, your rules of behavior for running Stonetop . Strive to follow them. They are the surest way to achieve your agenda. - Follow the rules - Begin and end with the fiction - Address th…

319.title: Follow the rules
319.breadcrumb: Examples of GM moves › Follow the rules
319.excerpt: There are many rules, especially in the form of player moves: basic moves and follower moves, special moves, exploration moves, and steading moves. Each playbook has moves. Inserts have moves. Arcana have moves. Dangers, discoveries, threat…

320.title: Begin and end with the fiction
320.breadcrumb: Examples of GM moves › Begin and end with the fiction
320.excerpt: Remember: "the fiction" is the shared, imagined space that you and the players establish through conversation, and the various characters' actions and relative positions within that imaginary space. When rules are invoked—when a player trig…

321.title: Address the characters, not the players
321.breadcrumb: Examples of GM moves › Address the characters, not the players
321.excerpt: Ask the characters what they do. Tell the characters what they see and hear. Tell the characters what's obvious to them. Don't say, "Brian, what is Vahid doing to keep awake?" Say instead: "Vahid, what are you doing to keep awake?" Don't te…

322.title: Ask questions and build on the answers
322.breadcrumb: Examples of GM moves › Ask questions and build on the answers
322.excerpt: Ask questions all the time, all over the place. Ask questions to establish intent . "Before you all head out in the morning, is there anything else anyone wants to do?" Ask questions to clarify intent and get yourself on the same page as th…

323.title: The Line
323.breadcrumb: Examples of GM moves › The Line
323.excerpt: In Stonetop and games like it, there's a line between what the players are responsible for and what you as the GM are responsible for. The players are in charge of their characters: what they do and say; what they feel and think; how they l…

324.title: Embrace the fantastic and the mundane
324.breadcrumb: Examples of GM moves › Embrace the fantastic and the mundane

325.title: Exploit the setting guide
325.breadcrumb: Examples of GM moves › Exploit the setting guide
325.excerpt: Stonetop comes with a lot of established setting, but it leaves a lot of blanks for you and your players to fill in during play. The setting is intended to serve as a springboard for your creativity. It helps set a tone and gives you all a…

326.title: Respect your prep
326.breadcrumb: Examples of GM moves › Respect your prep
326.excerpt: Between sessions, you'll make preparations (i.e. "prep"). You'll write up threats and NPCs. You'll create or choose dangers and discoveries. You'll think of encounters and design locations to explore. Some prep involves possibilities: thing…

327.title: Give your characters life
327.breadcrumb: Examples of GM moves › Give your characters life
327.excerpt: Name your NPCs. Give them distinct personalities. Wants, needs, and quirks. Opinions and beliefs. If you can manage it, portray them with distinct voices or mannerisms. Bring them to life and play them as if they were real people. Describe…

328.title: Think offscreen, too
328.breadcrumb: Examples of GM moves › Think offscreen, too
328.excerpt: When you establish the situation, answer questions, or make moves, think about events off-screen. What are your NPCs and monsters doing? What have they done? What traces did they leave? What have they been up to that becomes evident? What a…

329.title: Bring it home
329.breadcrumb: Examples of GM moves › Bring it home
329.excerpt: Make the village of Stonetop itself the foundation of your game, the beginning and end of the PCs' adventures. Threaten the town, its livelihood, or its citizens. Give them chances to make progress towards improvements, or to finally put do…

330.title: Let things breathe
330.breadcrumb: Examples of GM moves › Let things breathe
330.excerpt: Allow stretches of time to pass between adventures, or even between individual moves. It's a big world out there, and the PCs have mundane, daily tasks to attend to. Avoid the temptation to follow the PCs lives from day to day, hour to hour…

331.title: Let things burn
331.breadcrumb: Examples of GM moves › Let things burn
331.excerpt: As much as you like your plans, as much as you come to love some of your NPCs, as much as the players will grow attached: protect no one and nothing. Many GMs have a tendency, a very natural tendency, to coddle the PCs and to shield the NPC…

332.title: Other things to do
332.breadcrumb: Examples of GM moves › Other things to do
332.excerpt: Take notes. You'll end up generating lots of details, and it can be hard to keep track of it all. You'll need to figure out the best tool and the level of detail that works for you, but things you might want to write down include: - NPC nam…

333.title: The Die of Fate
333.breadcrumb: Examples of GM moves › The Die of Fate
333.excerpt: One way to disclaim decision making is to let the dice decide. Often, that means letting the results of a player's move determine what happens. But you can also use the Die of Fate. The Die of Fate is just a die roll, usually a d6, with a l…

334.title: What not to do
334.breadcrumb: Examples of GM moves › What not to do
334.excerpt: Don't plan a storyline or a plot. Don't plan the PCs' character arcs. Don't plan dramatic moments, where the PCs arrive just as this particular thing happens (regardless of how long they took to get there or what they did before). Don't ass…

335.title: GMing is a practice
335.breadcrumb: Examples of GM moves › GMing is a practice
335.excerpt: Running any RPG is a practice, like yoga or music or martial arts. It's not something that you master. There's always room for improvement, always something to learn. Running Stonetop specifically is its own skill, its own practice. Even if…

336.title: Prep
336.excerpt: Prep is what you do between sessions, the lonely fun (or for some, the stressful work) of getting ready for the next game. Prep involves reviewing your notes from previous sessions, thinking about what might happen in the next session, and…

337.title: Hooks

337.excerpt: When you want to push the PCs into the field with a threat or an opportunity, it pays to prepare a hook. A hook involves: setup questions, a plan for framing initial scenes, some specific GM moves you plan to make, and some possible reactio…

338.title: NPCs

338.excerpt: If there are NPCs that you expect they'll encounter in play, think about their names, the questions you'll ask about them, their defining traits, how you'll describe them and portray them. Maybe give them an instinct, connections, and motiv…

345.title: AID

346.title: CLASH

348.title: INTERFERE
348.excerpt: When you try to foil another PC's action and neither of you back down, roll... - ... +STR to power through or test your might - ... +DEX to employ speed, agility, or finesse - ... +CON to endure or hold steady - ... +INT to apply expertise…

349.title: KNOW THINGS
