350.title: LET FLY
350.excerpt: When you take an easy shot with a ranged weapon , deal your damage. If the shot is tricky or you're under pressure , first roll +DEX: on a 10+ , you have a clear shot, deal your damage; on a 7-9 , pick 1: - Deal your damage, but deplete you…

351.title: PERSUADE (vs. NPCs)
351.excerpt: When you press or entice an NPC , say what you want them to do (or not do). If they have reason to resist , roll +CHA: on a 10+ , they either do as you want or reveal the easiest way to convince them; on a 7-9 , they reveal something you ca…

352.title: PERSUADE (vs. PCs)
352.excerpt: When you press or entice a PC and they resist , ask their player: "Could I possibly get you to do this, yes or no?" If they say "no," let it drop. If they say "yes," you can roll +CHA: on a 10+ , they mark XP if they do what you want, and i…

353.title: Persuade or Interfere?
353.excerpt: Use Persuade when one PC tries to get another PC to agree to a course of action. Use Interfere when one PC has initiated a course of action, and another PC tries to stop or foil them. If one PC Persuades another, the other PC can Interfere…

355.title: Special moves
355.excerpt: These moves are always in play, but they either affect other moves or are triggered by very specific circumstances.

356.title: ADVANTAGE/DISADVANTAGE
356.excerpt: When you make a roll with advantage , roll an extra die and discard the lowest result. When you make a roll with disadvantage , roll an extra die and discard the highest result. When you make a roll with both advantage and disadvantage , th…

357.title: Adjusting difficulty
357.excerpt: In many RPGs, the GM routinely applies bonuses or penalties to player rolls to reflect the difficulty or ease of their tasks. In Stonetop , you can apply advantage or disadvantage "on the fly" to do the same thing, but do so sparingly—it un…

359.title: END OF SESSION
359.excerpt: When a session ends , point out how you demonstrated or struggled with your instinct. If you can, mark XP. Say how your relationship with or opinion of a PC, NPC, or group has changed. If you can, mark XP. Answer these questions as a group.…

368.title: Taking damage
368.excerpt: When a character takes damage, they reduce their current HP by that amount. If they are reduced to 0 HP, then they are out of the action. When a player character is reduced to 0 HP and the source of the damage was potentially lethal , then…

370.title: Debilities
370.excerpt: Player characters can suffer up to three different debilities: - Weakened : the character is fatigued, tired, sluggish, shaky. They suffer disad vantage when rolling +STR or +DEX. - Dazed : the character is out of it, be fuddled, not thinki…

371.excerpt: Players mark debilities on the character sheets to indicate that their character suffers from them. Player characters might mark a debility because... - … they trigger a player move, and that move says that they mark a debility; - … you hur…

372.title: Clearing debilities
372.excerpt: A player character can clear a debility when... - … they Recover , and explain how they're treating the debility; - … they Make Camp , and choose to clear a debility; - … they have a few days of downtime to Convalesce ; - … another move say…

374.title: Problematic wounds
374.excerpt: A problematic wound is one with lasting fictional consequences. Characters suffer them when you hurt them or make another GM move that involves a character getting hurt in a specific, consequential way. Simply describe the wound in the fict…

382.title: CONVALESCE
382.excerpt: When you rest for a few days in safety and comfort, set your HP back to your max and clear all your debilities. When you rest for a few weeks under the care of a healer , you heal any problematic wounds that can heal. If you have suffered a…

384.title: Goals
384.excerpt: Introductions are complete. Spring has sprung forth. You've got a rich tapestry of characters, relationships, and backstories. It's time for the first adventure! Let's put all that stuff into play and see the PCs in action. Your goals for t…

385.title: Paint a picture of daily life
385.breadcrumb: Goals › Paint a picture of daily life
385.excerpt: Your first adventure should start in Stonetop and help make it real. The setup and the initial scenes should show off mundane slices of life and reinforce the fact that this is the PCs' home—and that it's a home worth fighting for.

386.title: Explore relationships
386.breadcrumb: Goals › Paint a picture of daily life › Explore relationships
386.excerpt: Take those NPCs and relationships that were established during introductions and push them into the spotlight. How does Caradoc treat his sweetheart? How does Blodwen feel about Rhianna's promise to keep her safe? Don't just explore relatio…

387.title: Identify what's important to them
387.breadcrumb: Goals › Identify what's important to them
387.excerpt: There's nothing like a crisis to make someone's priorities come to the forefront. Keep an eye on the decisions that characters make, and where their interests lie. That'll be all sorts of fodder for later.

388.title: Push them into the field
388.breadcrumb: Goals › Push them into the field
388.excerpt: Whatever you do, don't spend very long in Stonetop itself. Get them into the field, on an adventure, as soon as possible. "Wait, what?" you might be thinking. "We just spent all that time establishing the village… aren't we going to DO some…

389.title: Establish details, leave blanks
389.excerpt: Both in the village and in the wider world, you want to be asking questions like crazy and establishing all sorts of concrete details. Who lives in those houses just outside the Ringwall? What flowers grow all around the old Fae waystones?…

390.title: Play up both the mundane and the mysterious
390.breadcrumb: Goals › Play up both the mundane and the mysterious
390.excerpt: Stonetop adventures should include a healthy dose of both mundane concerns and mysterious elements. If the adventure hook is mundane (like a chance to capture some wild horses), lace the adventure with strangeness and magic. If the hook is…

391.title: Draw them back home
391.excerpt: Whatever the first adventure is, it should end with them returning to Stonetop. This should happen pretty naturally, but avoid things like trapping the whole party in some Fae's demesne or having them follow a bunch of slavers to Lygos. Let…

392.title: Structure
392.excerpt: Regardless of whether you use an adventure starter or a custom adventure, the first adventure will generally follow this structure in play: - 1) Start with the setup , in which you ask a bunch of loaded questions that provide context and es…

393.title: Adventure starters

394.title: Background

395.title: Identify the central threat or opportunity
395.breadcrumb: Goals › Identify the central threat or opportunity
395.excerpt: The first adventure will center around an opportunity or a threat. The "spring bursts forth" roll (and the player's choice) at the end of introductions will tell you which.

396.title: On a 10+, an opportunity
396.excerpt: If they rolled a 10+ on their Seasons Change roll, they will have chosen "interesting news" or "valuable insight" or "trade opportunity." All three choices represent an opportunity. The opportunity should be both... - … external, so as to g…

397.title: On a 7-9, a threat and a gain
397.breadcrumb: Goals › On a 7-9, a threat and a gain
397.excerpt: If they rolled a 7-9, then a threat to the steading presents itself (or gets worse). But they also will have chosen a seasonal gain. The threat will be the main focus of the first adventure, but how it presents itself should be influenced b…

398.excerpt: Things are going to start bad and they're going to get worse. Start with a threat, one that came up during introductions. If no threats were established, or none of them feel imminent or appropriately bad, then pick something from an almana…

499.title: "… a corresponding amount of supplies"
499.breadcrumb: Expeditions › "… a corresponding amount of supplies"
499.excerpt: "Supplies" are something characters can have in their inventory. They represent stuff that the PCs might need or want on an expedition: food, water, whisky, bandages, salves, etc. The steading's Prosperity stat affects the quality and quant…

500.title: OUTFIT
500.excerpt: When you prepare for an expedition in a friendly community , mark as many on your Inventory insert as you wish to carry, either on specific items or in "undefined." Mark up to 3 for a light load (quick and quiet), 4-6 for a normal load, or…

501.title: REQUISITION
501.excerpt: When you borrow some of the steading's assets for an expedition (like the horses or a plow), roll +Fortunes: on a 10+ , go ahead, but bring it back safely; on a 7-9 , you'll need to do some convincing; on a 6- , don't mark XP—you can take t…

502.title: Other preparations
502.excerpt: These might happen before the PCs Outfit and Requisition or after, or sometimes in the middle of making choices. Zoom in and out as appropriate, switching between loose play and scenes. Gloss over the hours spent interviewing townsfolk, but…

503.title: Trading & bartering
503.excerpt: If the PCs want a special item for the trip—like bendis root, a nice lantern, or a bronze weapon—they'll need to Trade & Barter. This takes time. If they're in a rush or have other priorities before they leave, this won't be an option. See…

504.title: Gathering information
504.excerpt: The PCs might have questions they want or need answered before leaving. They could Know Things ("Has anything like this happened before?") or Seek Insight ("I go down and investigate the scene of the attack"). They might interview an NPC to…

505.title: Bringing NPCs and followers
505.excerpt: Established followers like the Marshal's crew will usually join an expedition if asked, but they might need convincing if the mission is foolish or against the follower's goals. Other NPCs might offer to join the PCs, or insist on doing so.…

506.title: Putting others to work
506.excerpt: Before they go, the PCs might set the townsfolk to work. For example: They could Muster (page 534), getting the town ready for trouble. Have them roll before leaving; they'll need to choose "the muster holds together even without your prese…

507.title: Miscellaneous
507.excerpt: Your players will certainly come up with other things to do before leaving. Follow the fiction and your principles. Zoom in and out as you see fit. Tie their actions back to the townsfolk and ask lots of questions. If they trigger a move th…

508.title: Flashbacks
508.excerpt: Sometimes, the PCs get into the field and a player realizes that they should have done something before they left. Let simple things go with, "Yeah, sure, of course." For more involved activities, consider playing it out as a flashback. Fra…

509.title: Running the journey
509.excerpt: The PCs have made their preparations. They're doing this. "We head out," they say. They're looking at you. Now what? Break the journey up into points of interest and legs of travel . The points of interest are the landmarks and the spots wh…

510.title: Legs of travel
510.excerpt: Look at the PCs' route and find the next point of interest—the next landmark or spot where you plan to frame a scene. Identify the path that the PCs will take to get there. That's the next leg of travel. If the leg of travel is trivial —une…

511.title: Frame the action
511.excerpt: Say where they find themselves, and how long they've been going (minutes, hours, days). "Okay, you head out? A half-hour later, you find yourselves past the Old Wall, stepping onto the West Road. An hour later, you're surrounded by the Flat…

512.title: If the leg goes through familiar terrain before—then just describe the current
512.breadcrumb: If the leg goes through familiar terrain before—then just describe the current
512.excerpt: an environment that the players have seen weather and maybe remind them what this terrain is like.

513.title: Ask questions
513.excerpt: Keep players engaged and create a sense of time passing as they travel by asking lots of questions . If the leg goes through unfamiliar terrain , then ask the characters questions about the terrain or what it's like to travel through it. Th…

514.title: If the leg goes through familiar terrain,
514.breadcrumb: Ask questions › If the leg goes through familiar terrain,
514.excerpt: you should still ask questions, but focus those questions on the weather, or what the PCs are thinking or talking about. Or suggest that they Keep Company , and let them ask questions of each other.

515.title: Portray NPCs If there are any NPCs on the journey,
515.breadcrumb: Ask questions › Portray NPCs If there are any NPCs on the journey,
515.excerpt: describe what they're up to, what they're talking about, how they're reacting to this leg of travel. Maybe ask players what they have their followers doing, or how they think their followers act.

516.title: Add details, answer questions, clarify the situation
516.breadcrumb: Ask questions › Add details, answer questions, clarify the situation
516.excerpt: Elaborate on the situation as much or as little as your players' interest demands. If they're engaged and enjoying themselves, asking questions and offering more details, then let things breathe and spend some time just talking about the le…

517.title: Make a soft GM move
517.breadcrumb: Ask questions › Add details, answer questions, clarify the situation › Make a soft GM move
517.excerpt: Once the situation is well-established, and you feel like it's time to nudge things along, make a soft GM move. - Present a challenge you listed when they Charted a Course - Present an encounter from your prep , or from the setting guide -…

518.title: Resolve their actions
518.breadcrumb: Ask questions › Add details, answer questions, clarify the situation › Make a soft GM move › Resolve their actions
518.excerpt: Ask them what they do. If they trigger a player move, resolve it. If they roll a 6-, or if they ignore an established threat, then make a hard GM move. Otherwise, say what happens. A player's action might prompt you to zoom in and frame a s…

519.title: Repeat
519.breadcrumb: Ask questions › Add details, answer questions, clarify the situation › Repeat
519.excerpt: Have you zoomed in and framed a scene? Cool. Play out the scene—establishing the situation, making soft GM moves, asking "what do you do?" and resolving their actions—until the scene is finished. Then move on to the next scene or situation,…

520.title: Points of interest
520.excerpt: A point of interest is either... - ... a landmark that has not yet been seen in play (or that has changed since the PCs' last visit); - ... a specific place where you plan to frame a scene and make a GM move; or - ... the journey's destinat…

521.title: Frame the action
521.excerpt: Establish the PCs arriving at the point of interest. Say when they get there. Zoom in, make it a scene. Normally, you'll frame the scene with the PCs at the point of interest, having already arrived. But if you want to build tension because…

522.title: Describe the environment
522.excerpt: If this place is unfamiliar to the players , then describe it. What makes it distinct from the surrounding terrain? What are its obvious, defining features? Provide up to 3 impressions using different senses (see the entries in Book II for…

523.title: Ask questions
523.breadcrumb: Ask questions › Describe the environment › Ask questions
523.excerpt: If this is the first time the players visit a known landmark in play (like the Stream or the Crossroads), ask the characters questions about the place. The Book II entries for each locale include questions you might ask, and you can always…

524.title: Portray NPCs If there are any NPCs on the journey,
524.breadcrumb: Ask questions › Portray NPCs If there are any NPCs on the journey,
524.excerpt: ask yourself how they'd react to this place. Drop this into your descriptions to remind the players (and yourself ) that they're here. Maybe get ready to make a soft GM move with (or against) an NPC.

525.title: Add details, answer questions, clarify the scene
525.breadcrumb: Ask questions › Add details, answer questions, clarify the scene
525.excerpt: Flesh out the scene as much as you need to, in response to player questions or PC actions. Draw a quick map or picture if you think it will help. Figure out who's where and doing what. If there's confusion or disagreement about what's going…

526.title: Resolve their actions, repeat
526.breadcrumb: Ask questions › Add details, answer questions, clarify the scene › Resolve their actions, repeat
526.excerpt: Play out the scene. Ask them what they do. Resolve their actions. Establish how the scene and the situation has changed. Repeat, moving the spotlight around between PCs. Play to find out what happens . If this point of interest is their des…

527.title: Exploration moves
527.excerpt: Add these to your arsenal of GM moves once the players leave town and head into the wider world. These same moves get used when exploring sites , but here, we'll discuss how to use them as part of a journey. Your exploration moves are: - <s…

528.title: <sup>ä</sup> Provide a choice of paths
528.breadcrumb: Ask questions › <sup>ä</sup> Provide a choice of paths
528.excerpt: Present the proverbial fork in the road and ask them which way they go. There might be other paths, and they might do something different, but you're presenting the obvious options and prompting them to choose. Try to give them some indicat…

529.title: <sup>ä</sup> Hint at more than meets the eye
529.breadcrumb: Ask questions › <sup>ä</sup> Hint at more than meets the eye
529.excerpt: Drop a hint that there's something interesting here, a discovery to be discovered or a lie to be uncovered. This move often spurs the players to Seek Insight or Know Things or otherwise look closer. To make this move, point out something ou…

530.title: <sup>ä</sup> Offer riches at a price
530.breadcrumb: Ask questions › <sup>ä</sup> Offer riches at a price
530.excerpt: Show them something valuable, but costly or difficult to get. Then sit back and see if they think it's worth it. Consider "riches" to be anything the PCs would consider valuable. That could be actual riches, or just some sort of supply or r…

531.title: <sup>ä</sup> Present a discovery
531.excerpt: Discoveries are interesting things that the PCs find but that aren't inherently or immediately dangerous. They might be useful or valuable, or just something mysterious that's worth investigating. See the Discoveries chapter, starting on Di…

532.title: <sup>ä</sup> Point to a looming danger
532.breadcrumb: Ask questions › <sup>ä</sup> Point to a looming danger
532.excerpt: This is the claw-print in the mud, the wolves howling in the distance. This is the piles of sticks, strangely neat and tidy, sure sign that a hagr dwells nearby. Use this move to foreshadow future dangers, to make the PCs nervous and to pro…

533.title: <sup>ä</sup> Introduce a danger, person, or faction
533.breadcrumb: Ask questions › <sup>ä</sup> Introduce a danger, person, or faction
533.excerpt: With this move, you're putting a danger on screen. You're not pointing to it, it's not looming, it's here , what do you do? Alternately, you're introducing an NPC and giving the PCs a chance to interact with them. Or, you're revealing that…

534.title: <sup>ä</sup> Bar the way
534.excerpt: They encounter an obstacle, or hit a dead end, or discover they lack something needed to move ahead. They've got to find a way through, or over, or to backtrack and find a way around. Describe what's barring the way forward, and maybe give…

535.title: Use other moves, too!
535.breadcrumb: Ask questions › Use other moves, too!
535.excerpt: Just to be clear: you can and should use your standard GM moves while the PCs are on a journey. Ask provocative questions ("what's your crew doing to get on your nerves?"), demonstrate downsides ("you're falling behind under all that gear")…

536.title: Resolving Chart a Course
536.breadcrumb: Ask questions › Resolving Chart a Course
536.excerpt: When the PCs Charted a Course, you gave them a list of requirements and challenges (see Chart a Course).

537.title: Requirements
537.excerpt: Hopefully, they addressed the requirements (like "You'll need to bring \ \ " or "You need a knowledgeable guide") before they left. If they didn't, tell them the consequences of not being prepared. If they persist, either bar the way or add…

538.title: Presenting challenges
538.excerpt: Once the PCs head out on the journey, you need to present the challenges that you warned them about, plus any others that they couldn't have seen coming. That includes both surprises that you've prepped in advance, or things that arise natu…

539.title: "The way is perilous, filled with dangers"
539.breadcrumb: Ask questions › Presenting challenges › "The way is perilous, filled with dangers"
539.excerpt: This isn't a specific challenge, it's more of an ongoing threat. Save it for truly dangerous legs of travel: Ferrier's Fen, the Steplands near the lakes, the Labyrinth, etc. On each leg of perilous travel, and each time they Make Camp in a…

540.title: 1d6 danger
540.excerpt: - 1 A danger springs on them, unavoidable - 2-3 Introduce a danger, right in front of them - 4-5 Point to a looming danger - 6 Point to a looming danger, but also present a discovery Also, feel free to spring dangers on them any time that t…

541.title: Weather
541.excerpt: The weather is an important part of any journey. It colors the whole trip and can be a challenge all by itself. You decide when it rains and when the sun shines, based on your agenda and principles but also on your sense of theme and story.…

548.title: 1d6 weather 1d6 weather
548.excerpt: - 4-6 Crisp, breezy 5 Some snow, but mostly just dreary - 6 Warm (for winter) and sunny

549.title: Player moves
549.excerpt: These moves become relevant while the PCs are on an expedition.

550.title: HAVE WHAT YOU NEED
550.breadcrumb: Weather tables › HAVE WHAT YOU NEED
550.excerpt: When you decide that you had some-thing all along , transfer a mark (or marks) from your "undefined" inventory to a specific item or a slot. If you mark a slot, fill it with a common mundane item or something from your special possessions.…

551.title: Loot, load, and inventory
551.breadcrumb: Weather tables › Loot, load, and inventory
551.excerpt: If a PC or follower acquires something in the field (like loot) and wants to bring it along, they have to add it to their inventory. Small items (things that could fit in a pocket, purse, or boot with room to spare) don't count against a ch…

552.title: RECOVER
552.excerpt: When you take time to catch your breath and tend to what ails you , expend 1 use of supplies and regain HP equal to 4+Prosperity. You can't gain this benefit again until you take more damage. When you tend to a debility or a problematic wou…

553.title: STRUGGLE AS ONE
553.excerpt: When you Defy Danger as a group , establish the party's approach and each roll +STAT (per Defy Danger): on a 6- , you find yourself in a spot, the GM will describe it or ask you to; on a 7-9 , you pull your weight; on a 10+ , you do well en…

554.title: KEEP COMPANY
554.excerpt: When you spend a stretch of time together, ask the others if they want to Keep Company. If they do, take turns asking a PC or NPC one of the following. - What do you do that's annoying/ endearing? - What do I do that you find annoying/endea…

555.title: MAKE CAMP
555.excerpt: When you settle in to rest in an unsafe area , answer the GM's questions about your campsite. Each member of the party must consume 1 use of supplies or provisions; if you use a mess kit ( requires fire & water ), then 1 use can provide for…

556.title: 1d6 occurrence
556.excerpt: - 1 Something dangerous approaches, inclined to do harm. - 2 Something dangerous approaches, curious but not aggressive. - 3 Something annoying happens (critters in the food, rain, an argument, etc.). - 4-5 The night passes uneventfully. -…

557.title: Deprivation
557.excerpt: If PCs don't get food, drink, or rest, then at first the only consequence is that they don't get to make a choice when they Make Camp. But the longer they go with insufficient food, water, or rest, the more danger they're in. Start by askin…

558.title: FORAGE
558.excerpt: When you spend a few hours seeking food in the wild , roll +WIS. In winter, you have disadvantage. On a 10+ , pick 2; on a 7-9 , pick 1: - You acquire <sup 4</sup provisions (1d6 uses) - You acquire an extra 1d6 uses of provisions - You dis…

559.title: Going home
559.excerpt: The PCs will eventually turn back and head for home. Now what?

560.title: The return journey
560.excerpt: In general, gloss over the trip home. The points of interest that you described on the way out probably haven't changed much. The party already faced the challenges you established when they Charted a Course. The trip home might still be lo…

561.title: Arriving home
561.excerpt: Before the PCs arrive home, consider: - How long have they been gone, and how has their absence been felt? - If they suffered casualties, who back home will be most affected or upset? - What have folks back home been up to? - If the PCs Req…

562.title: RETURN TRIUMPHANT
562.excerpt: When you return home in triumph , having saved your fellows, put down the threat, seized the opportunity, etc., then clear one of the steading's debilities ( diminished , lacking , or malcontent ). If the steading has no debilities marked,…

563.title: What if they… don't?
563.breadcrumb: Weather tables › What if they… don't?
563.excerpt: It's possible that the PCs might not want to return home. They might want to continue exploring an area, or resettle in a different town, or just leave and never come back. To help prevent this, remember to bring it home . While the PCs are…

564.title: What to prep
564.excerpt: If you know that an expedition is in the PCs' future, there's plenty you can prepare in advance. You don't need to prepare these things, but prep can help make the journey go more smoothly in play. First, chart the course . Look at where th…

565.title: Identify your points of interest, including…
565.breadcrumb: Weather tables › Identify your points of interest, including…
565.excerpt: - … any landmark that has not yet been seen in play (or that has changed since the PCs' last visit); - … any specific places where you plan to frame a scene and make a GM move; and... - … the journey's destination. Then identify the legs of…

566.title: Sites
566.excerpt: Environmental storytelling Running sites Exploring sites Creating sites The Green Lord's Tomb Sites are interesting places that the PCs can explore. They might be the destination of an expedition, a point of interest along the way, or just…

567.title: Environmental storytelling
567.excerpt: Sites have stories to tell. You should be able to answer questions like: - Who built this place? - How did it fall into ruin or disuse? - Who or what dwells here now? - When and why did they arrive? - What are they up to now? - What else ha…

568.title: "What here tells you...?"
568.excerpt: Consider asking your players to paint the scene for you. Identify a detail or element of the site's story and ask, "What here tells you \ \ ?" Let them do the environmental storytelling. For example, "What here tells you that… - … this plac…

569.title: Running sites
569.excerpt: When the PCs explore a site, you run the game normally and follow the core loop . You play out a series of scenes and situations, as always. They just happen to take place in that site.

570.title: Considerations
570.excerpt: As PCs explore sites—as you establish the situation, make GM moves, and resolve PC actions—keep in mind:

571.title: The physical space
571.excerpt: Its rough dimensions, its construction, the height of ceilings and width of passages, obstacles, footing, slopes, different levels. You can use maps to help describe and clarify the site's layout and where things are.

572.title: Light sources
572.excerpt: Who's carrying them? What are their tags? How far can they see? What out in the dark can see them? How long will their light sources last, and is that long enough?

573.title: Senses other than sight
573.excerpt: Sounds of course, but also odors and textures, heat and cold and humidity, air pressure and movement.

574.title: Marching order
574.breadcrumb: Senses other than sight › Marching order
574.excerpt: Who's going first, who's next, and who's bringing up the rear? This is especially important in cramped quarters or when something attacks.

575.title: Equipment
575.breadcrumb: Senses other than sight › Marching order › Equipment
575.excerpt: What does everyone have in hand, or at the ready? How heavy a load are they carrying? If it's a heavy load, how much does that suck? Do they have room to swing that awkward maul in here? Can they fit through that tunnel with their shield?

576.title: Followers and NPCs
576.breadcrumb: Senses other than sight › Marching order › Equipment › Followers and NPCs
576.excerpt: Include them in the situation! Where are they? What are they doing? How do they react to \ \ ? Make GM moves through them, or at them. If left behind at camp or to stand guard, what (if anything) befalls them?

577.title: Physical and mental states
577.breadcrumb: Senses other than sight › Marching order › Equipment › Physical and mental states
577.excerpt: Hunger and thirst, fatigue, irritability, disorientation, dread. How do the PCs react? How do NPCs handle it? (Spoiler: not well.)

578.title: Denizens
578.breadcrumb: Senses other than sight › Marching order › Equipment › Denizens
578.excerpt: Who or what else is here? What signs of their presence might the PCs find? Would they notice the PCs? If so, how would they react? Are they hostile or wary or curious, or what?

579.title: Light sources & tags
579.excerpt: Here's a quick reference: Hand : E.g., a rushlight. Illuminates no farther than your reach. Barely enough to navigate or read by. Close, area : E.g., a candle, oil lamp, or lantern. Casts light a few feet, enough for 2 people to navigate by…

580.title: Exploring sites
580.excerpt: Most sites are made up of multiple discrete areas or rooms. Some sites are so big that these areas or rooms are divided into zones or smaller sites. As the PCs explore a site, you'll zoom in on one of these discrete places, establish a scen…

581.title: GM exploration moves
581.breadcrumb: Light sources & tags › GM exploration moves
581.excerpt: Use these GM moves heavily as the PCs explore a site. These are the same moves that you use while running a journey , but here we'll discuss using them in more local, focused areas. Your exploration moves are: - <sup ä</sup Provide a choice…

582.title: <sup>ä</sup> Provide a choice of paths
582.breadcrumb: Light sources & tags › <sup>ä</sup> Provide a choice of paths
582.excerpt: Use this move to prompt the PCs to move on, signaling that there's nothing more of interest here. Use to it remind them of their options when they decide to move on themselves. Or, use it to force a decision. The choice of paths should be m…

583.title: <sup>ä</sup> Hint at more than meets the eye
583.breadcrumb: Light sources & tags › <sup>ä</sup> Hint at more than meets the eye
583.excerpt: Think about something you want to reveal to the PCs, but that wouldn't be obvious. Then think about what telltale signs could point to it. Describe that. See what the PCs do. They'll often ask questions, Know Things, or Seek Insight—or all…

584.title: <sup>ä</sup> Offer riches at a price
584.breadcrumb: Light sources & tags › <sup>ä</sup> Offer riches at a price
584.excerpt: Present something valuable to the PCs be it actual treasure, information, a needed supply, etc.—but also present a danger that makes it costly or difficult to get. See if they think it's worth it. "There's this beautiful wooden chest up aga…

585.title: <sup>ä</sup> Present a discovery
585.breadcrumb: Light sources & tags › <sup>ä</sup> Present a discovery
585.excerpt: Pick a discovery—a clue, an encounter, an opportunity, an artifact or arcanum, or even another, smaller site. Maybe roll on a table from Book II to decide what it is. Put it in front of the PCs, and see what they do. Refer to the Discoverie…

586.title: <sup>ä</sup> Point to a looming danger
586.breadcrumb: Light sources & tags › <sup>ä</sup> Point to a looming danger
586.excerpt: Use this move to telegraph a threat, a monster, or a hazard, to reveal and reflect its presence in the site. "What should you be on the lookout for? Um… oh. You find some scat, like fistsized, and ropey, full of fur and bones. Whatever this…

587.title: <sup>ä</sup> Introduce a danger, person, or faction
587.breadcrumb: Light sources & tags › <sup>ä</sup> Introduce a danger, person, or faction
587.excerpt: Put a danger directly in the PCs' way. As a hard move, have it catch them unawares. Or, have them encounter a denizen of the site, or a group thereof. The line between "person" and "danger" isn't always clear. "Standing by the pool, so stil…

588.title: <sup>ä</sup> Bar the way
588.breadcrumb: Light sources & tags › <sup>ä</sup> Bar the way
588.excerpt: Make this move to deny access to part of a site. It could be something that's been there all along, like a locked door or a chasm. Or you could cut the PCs off as they explore, via a cave-in, a trap, a bridge collapsing behind them, etc. Ma…

589.title: Use other moves, too!
589.breadcrumb: Light sources & tags › Use other moves, too!
589.excerpt: Don't forget to use your standard GM moves while the PCs explore a site, as well as threat moves and moves from hazards and monsters. For example: - <sup ä</sup Announce trouble: "You hear footsteps approaching." - <sup ä</sup Use up their…

590.title: Creating sites
590.excerpt: You won't always create sites the same way, or to the same level of detail. Sometimes, you'll improvise a site in play; other times, you'll carefully prep them between sessions. Often, you'll do a bit of both. Regardless of how much you're…

591.title: 1) Lay the foundation
591.excerpt: - Ask questions - Build on what you've got - Exploit the setting guide

592.title: 2) Build up its story
592.breadcrumb: Creating sites › 1) Lay the foundation › 2) Build up its story
592.excerpt: - Look for connections - Ask yourself questions - Answer those questions - Create a timeline

593.title: 3) Sketch out the site's contents
593.breadcrumb: Creating sites › 1) Lay the foundation › 2) Build up its story › 3) Sketch out the site's contents
593.excerpt: - Populate it - Identify dangers and discoveries - Establish areas/rooms - Describe the environment - Arrange areas/rooms

594.title: 4)Write it up as much as you like
594.breadcrumb: Creating sites › 1) Lay the foundation › 2) Build up its story › 4)Write it up as much as you like
594.excerpt: - Create a map or visual (maybe) - Detail each area/room - Subdivide as needed - Make lists and tables - Make plans - Review and revise You don't need to do this entire process, or do it all at once. If pressed for time, do only as much of…

595.title: 1 Foundation
595.excerpt: Start with a core concept for the site and its story, something with a few compelling details that you can build on while improvising or as you continue to prep. This should be brief: a few sentences and an evocative title, no more.

596.title: Ask questions
596.excerpt: If you're establishing the site during play, and the PCs might know about it, defer to them. Has a PC been here before? Ask what it's like! Would a PC have heard of it? Ask what they've heard. As always, incorporate the players' answers int…

597.title: Build on what you've got
597.breadcrumb: Creating sites › Build on what you've got
597.excerpt: Think through what you know about this site. What can you assume from context? What have you told the players and what have they told you? What have you already decided, in your heart of hearts? If there's a resource that you want to build…

598.title: Exploit the setting guide
598.breadcrumb: Creating sites › Exploit the setting guide
598.excerpt: Book II is full of example sites and tools to help you create your own. Use it for inspiration and to help make sites that fit the larger Stonetop setting. If you're making a site from scratch, start by deciding what manner of site it is: a…

599.title: THE GREEN LORD'S TOMB
599.breadcrumb: Creating sites › THE GREEN LORD'S TOMB
599.excerpt: A dome-shaped tomb, mostly intact, with a few old oaks growing out of it. Looks like a small hill, with bits of worked stone poking out of the soil. Inside are wide-open halls of white marble with intricate scrollwork, leading to the still-…

600.title: 2 Story
600.excerpt: Sites should tell stories, even if those stories are relatively simple. A site's story informs how you describe it and what the PCs might find within. If you're prepping between sessions , then give this process some time. Take notes. Brows…

601.title: Look for connections
601.excerpt: Start with the site's foundation , the details already established, and the questions you've started asking yourself. Consider how those things connect to: - Each other - Other setting elements in Book II - The setting's timeline (see ages…

602.title: Ask yourself questions
602.excerpt: Think about what you've got so far, and start asking questions. Write them down! Ask yourself about… - … the connections you've made and what those connections imply. "What do the Ustrina know about this ruin? Why do they stay away?" - … an…

603.title: Answer those questions
603.excerpt: Start answering your questions, in whatever order makes sense or whatever order the answers occur to you. If there's a clear answer to a question , then great, use that. Otherwise, jot down some possible answers. Either pick between them ra…

604.title: Create a timeline
604.excerpt: As the site's story becomes clear to you, start to create a timeline of key events. Put them in a relative order: this happened, then this, and then this. Vague times are usually fine (centuries ago, decades ago, years ago, last winter, las…

605.title: If you're prepping the site in advance,
605.breadcrumb: Creating sites › If you're prepping the site in advance,
605.excerpt: then write this timeline down in as much detail as you find useful. It's typically a bullet list of key events, but you can include details like the motives or experiences of actors in the site's story. If you're so inclined, you might even…

606.title: If you're improvising a site during play,
606.breadcrumb: Creating sites › If you're improvising a site during play,
606.excerpt: then keep things high-level. Focus on the details relevant to play. If you're creating a Stone Lord ruin that's been haunted by a ghost for a few decades, then gloss over the ruin's origins and spend more time on the ghost's death and subse…

607.title: IN THE TIME OF THE MAKERS (millennia ago)
607.breadcrumb: Creating sites › IN THE TIME OF THE MAKERS (millennia ago)
607.excerpt: - Tomb is built, Green Lord interred - Centuries later: Fae revolt starts! Guardian Fae stay loyal, but rebels ransack the tomb's outer tunnels, burn most mummified servants - Thornthumb displaced by war; finds tomb, moves in to outer tunne…

608.title: OVER THE NEXT FEW CENTURIES
608.breadcrumb: Creating sites › IN THE TIME OF THE MAKERS (millennia ago) › OVER THE NEXT FEW CENTURIES
608.excerpt: - Forest Folk sometimes petition Green Lord's spirit for knowledge/aid - Thornthumb collects tolls from Forest Folk petitioners; his hoard grows

609.title: LAST TWENTY YEARS OR SO
609.breadcrumb: Creating sites › IN THE TIME OF THE MAKERS (millennia ago) › LAST TWENTY YEARS OR SO
609.excerpt: - Rhianna & pa find burnt wolf here - Forest Folk disappear; crinwin and nailadd populations grow - Sajra is hatched in Vor Svetelik

610.title: LAST AUTUMN
610.excerpt: - Sajra arrives at tomb with crinwin thralls in tow; makes Thornthumb, promise to stay away from the tomb, Sajra, its servants and possessions - Sajra enthralls local crinwin; they make nests outside the tomb

611.title: LAST WINTER INTO SPRING
611.breadcrumb: LAST AUTUMN › LAST WINTER INTO SPRING
611.excerpt: - Crinwin kidnap Tiwlip from the Woods - Crinwin raid Stonetop, kidnap Wynfor and bring him back to the tomb - Since thaw: crinwin have started excavating other ruins near tomb

612.title: NOW
612.excerpt: • Crinwin have kidnapped Pryder, are dragging him towards the tomb.

613.title: 3 Contents
613.excerpt: Decide what the PCs might find as they explore the site: its inhabitants, the areas within it, the dangers and discoveries that make it interesting, and the connective tissue that holds it all together. If you're improvising the site during…

614.title: Populate it
614.excerpt: Who or what lives or dwells here? Who or what is visiting, exploring, or just passing through? You probably already know this, but make a list. Don't limit yourself to people. Think about beasts, spirits, constructs, undead entities, and an…

615.title: Identify dangers & discoveries
615.excerpt: Make a list of the dangers and discoveries implied by the site's foundation, story, and occupants. This might include: - Hazards that the PCs might need to deal with - Monsters , i.e. denizens that the PCs might fight - Clues , especially t…

616.title: Establish areas/rooms
616.excerpt: Write out the distinct rooms or places that are suggested by your site's foundation and story, by its denizens, and by common sense. A small site might have just one area, but most sites have at least a few. If the site was built for a spec…

617.title: Describe the environment
617.excerpt: Envision the site, or the individual area or room you're working on. Make some notes as to how you'll describe it. For a larger site, think in generalities. What sorts of rooms, areas, terrain, and features can be found there? Build out a v…

621.excerpt: - Intact inner tomb (burial vault, the wards, the guardian Fae) - Green Lord's afterlife (Fae domain) - Entrance hall (overgrown, crumbling, vandalized) - Exterior of tomb ("hill," oaks with the shabby nests of "new" crinwin). - Sajra's qua…

627.title: 4 Write-up
627.excerpt: Once you've identified the site's contents, organize your notes and write things up in as much detail as you find useful, valuable, or fun. The goal here is a document that helps you run the site in play . And as you create that document, y…

629.title: Detail each area/room
629.breadcrumb: Create maps or visuals (maybe) › Detail each area/room
629.excerpt: Give each area or room a title, something you can refer to in your notes: "Entryway" or "Long hall" or "room A," etc. Then write out the details and decisions that you want to remember about that area—the stuff you don't want to forget or t…

630.title: Subdivide as needed
630.breadcrumb: Create maps or visuals (maybe) › Subdivide as needed
630.excerpt: If there are details that you find yourself writing for multiple areas or rooms, pull that out into its own entry. Likewise, if certain dangers, discoveries, or NPCs might be found throughout the site, or are complex enough to warrant it, t…

631.title: Find/create content
631.breadcrumb: Create maps or visuals (maybe) › Find/create content
631.excerpt: If you need to add something to an area or the site as a whole, either pick something from Book II (and tweak it as needed) or create it from scratch. Refer to: - Preparing hazards - Monsters - Prepping clues - Building encounters - Opportu…

632.title: Make lists and tables
632.breadcrumb: Create maps or visuals (maybe) › Make lists and tables
632.excerpt: Some content exists in a quantum state. The sentries walk a perimeter. The cave bears might be out foraging. The ruin is vast and you can't be bothered to plot out where each danger, discovery, or NPC is at every moment. Put this sort of co…

633.title: Make plans
633.breadcrumb: Create maps or visuals (maybe) › Make plans
633.excerpt: Think about how NPCs or monsters are likely to react to the PCs, or what might happen when the PCs enter an area, or how long after X will Y happen. Reflect this in your notes as… - … a descriptive sentence or two; - … one or more GM moves…

634.excerpt: When you think you're done, read through your write-up. Is anything missing? Add it. Is anything no longer needed? Cut it. Make sure it makes sense. What was the site originally built or used for? Would it have served that purpose? How is i…

652.title: Dangers
652.excerpt: Hazards Monsters Running combat Dangers are likely to hurt the PCs or the things they care about. There are two basic types of danger: Hazards are environmental dangers, like traps, treacherous terrain, or weather. Monsters are active dange…

653.title: Dangers vs. threats
653.excerpt: Dangers may or may not also be threats (see Writing a threat). Threats are ongoing problems, things that will come back to haunt the PCs later; they might not be dangerous, but they're always troublesome. Dangers are immediate problems: thi…

654.title: Hazards
654.excerpt: Hazards are environmental dangers. They can impede or harm the PCs but lack initiative and intent. Hazards can't really be fought and slain with spear and shield; they must be avoided, endured, thwarted, or overcome.

655.title: Improvising a hazard
655.excerpt: Hazards don't always need game mechanics or stats. You'll often just improvise them. To improvise a hazard: imagine a physical obstacle or danger that would make sense in the environment. Then, frame a scene (if you haven't already) and mak…

656.title: Damage from hazards
656.excerpt: If a hazard (improvised or prepared) deals damage , ask yourself what it could likely do to a normal person. worst outcome die ------------------------------------------ --- Bruises & scrapes, pain, light burns d4 Nasty flesh wounds, bruise…

657.title: Preparing hazards
657.excerpt: If you're preparing an expedition or a point of interest in advance, you might want to prepare some hazards. Prep hazards for all the reasons you prep anything: to give yourself interesting stuff to say, to speed up play, to shore up your w…

658.title: As a detailed description
658.excerpt: Think about the hazard and its specific fictional details: Where is it? What does it look like? How does it work? What triggers it? What does it do? You might just visualize it in your mind, or jot some notes down, or even draw a picture or…

659.title: As GM moves
659.excerpt: Think about how the hazard works and more importantly—how you intend to use it in play. Write out a series of fairly specific GM moves that reflect as many of the following as make sense: - How the hazard's presence is fore shadowed or reve…

660.title: As an impending doom
660.excerpt: Write a series of increasingly bad events, ending with some final doom. Start by imagining the final, ultimate bad thing that can happen and write a short description, like "The throne room collapses." Then, write 1-4 grim portents, events…

661.title: As player moves
661.excerpt: Write a custom player-facing move to reflect the hazard (or part of it), like "When you breathe in the horrible stench of the Maw , roll +CON…." When the PCs encounter the hazard and the move becomes relevant, share it with the players. Res…

662.title: As a combination
662.excerpt: Many hazards will work best as a combination of these approaches. You might start with a detailed description of a trapped chamber, then add a player-facing move for what happens when they try to bypass the trap in a particular way. Or you…

663.breadcrumb: Dangers › When you enter the Hall of Humility, choose 1:

664.title: Monster format
664.excerpt: Almanac entries in The Wider World include many monster write-ups like this—use them when you can, either as-is or slightly modified. - 0 Icon : A quick visual identifier for different monster types (see Monster types). You don't need to in…

665.title: Monsters
665.excerpt: Monsters are active dangers—things that the PCs can fight (or at least fight off ). They are usually animate and sentient, or close enough. It's a game term more than anything else, covering beasts and people, constructs and spirits, and ac…

666.title: Monster types
666.excerpt: These icons are used to classify the different creatures appearing in Book II: The Wider World and Other Wonders . human individual spirit/construct Maker human group Fae emanation spirit corrupted/ Fomoraij unknown origin construct

667.title: 1 Concept
667.excerpt: Before you do anything else, imagine the monster. What is it? What does it look like? What does it do? Does it live alone or with others? How does it fight, eat, and live? You'll usually have some ideas for the monster when you start this p…

668.title: Tags
668.excerpt: Assemble a list of tags by answering the following questions. How does it hunt or fight? (pick 1) tag ----------------------------------- ---------- In large groups (6 or more) horde In small groups (2-5 per group) group By itself solitary…

669.excerpt: for each of the following that the monster possesses. - An exceptional/limited sense (like "sees heat instead of light") - A useful adaptation/defense (like "im mune to fire") - A strange form or composition (like "amorphous ooze" or "made…

670.breadcrumb: Dangers › <sup>10</sup> Description Write as much or as little of a
670.excerpt: description of your monster as you like. Remember, the audience here is you, using the monster during play. Write your description with that in mind. Think about how you'll describe the monster to your players during play. How big is it, wh…

671.breadcrumb: Dangers › <sup>11</sup> Optional elements If the monster has nuanced mo-
671.excerpt: tivations or behaviors, consider writing out tactics for the monster, as a series of "if-then" conditions and behaviors. This can help you portray your characters with integrity , and speed up play. To write tactics, think about how and whe…

674.title: Running combat
674.excerpt: Remember, "monster" is just a term for something that the PCs might end up fighting. It includes hostile (or potentially hostile) people as well as spirits and beasts and honest-to-goodness monsters . Regardless of their nature, monsters ha…

675.title: Portray your characters with integrity.
675.breadcrumb: Dangers › Portray your characters with integrity.
675.excerpt: Consider what this monster—with this instinct and these motives, facing these PCs who are acting in this way—would do. Then have it do that. Hint at the monster's story and its inner life. Present monsters who might not actually want to fig…

676.title: But also, portray monsters as monsters.
676.breadcrumb: Dangers › But also, portray monsters as monsters.
676.excerpt: Try to capture the fear of facing down a hungry predator or a big angry beast. Play up the tension, desperation, and bloody consequences of a fight with another human being. Think about what it'd be like to encounter a hungry ghost, or an o…

677.title: Introducing monsters
677.excerpt: Whenever it's time to make a GM move, you can introduce a danger and put a monster in the scene. Don't worry about your monsters being "fair fights" or "balanced encounters" or something that the PCs can even defeat. Worry about your monste…

678.title: The flow of battle
678.excerpt: Fighting foes is a big part of the game, but combat isn't a distinct mode of play. There's no moment where you say "roll for initiative" and different rules kick in. There's no orderly round-robin where everyone takes one action on their tu…

679.title: Foes they can't hurt
679.excerpt: Sometimes, the PCs can't feasibly attack their foe. The monster might have a special quality (like "made of stone") or tag (like huge ) that makes them effectively invulnerable to the PCs' weapons. It might have a move (like "swat arrows fr…

680.title: Fighting spirits
680.excerpt: Spirits are hard to fight because, by default, they lack a physical form. Most mundane attacks have no effect and pass right through them. And yet the most dangerous spirits can still attack physical creatures, even without manifesting! Som…

681.title: Multiple combatants
681.excerpt: When the PCs face multiple foes (and they often will), break up the action into multiple smaller engagements—the Ranger fights one crinwin, the Fox fights another, the Marshal and his crew deal with the rest of them. This isn't anything for…

682.title: Abstracting groups
682.excerpt: If the PCs have a group of followers, then you'll often want to abstract their actions in combat. Resolve the group's actions with as few individual moves as possible. For example, if the Marshal's crew of six opens fire on a horde of 20 cr…

683.title: Keeping fights interesting
683.excerpt: Fights should be exciting, dynamic, and tense. The players should never feel like they're just "trading blows" or trying to deplete a foe's HP. Players shouldn't get bored waiting for "their turn," and the outcome should never be entirely c…

684.title: Make soft GM moves all the damn time.
684.breadcrumb: Dangers › Make soft GM moves all the damn time.
684.excerpt: After every player move, describe the situation and make a soft move: say something that provokes action or raises the tension. Then ask someone, "What do you do?" Sometimes, your soft move will be offering an opportunity for a PC to act fr…

685.title: Consider the momentum of the action
685.breadcrumb: Dangers › Consider the momentum of the action
685.excerpt: and other elements of fictional positioning. Incorporate them into your descriptions and your GM moves. So if your soft move was "it swoops at your face, talons out" and their response was "I brace myself and drive my long spear into its gu…

686.title: Maps, tokens, and minis
686.excerpt: It's often helpful to visually represent a battle, especially one with lots of combatants and distinct zones. This could be anything from a crude sketch of the battlefield to a fullfledged diorama with 3D minis and terrain. Visualizations l…

687.title: Discoveries
687.excerpt: Clues Sites Encounters Opportunities Artifacts Arcana Discoveries are the interesting things that PCs encounter, stuff that isn't immediately dangerous but that prompts investigation, interaction, and decisions. They include: - Clues , like…

688.title: Clues
688.excerpt: Clues point the PCs towards some sort of knowledge, conclusion, or revelation. They are signifiers of something else. They are nouns that tell stories. Present clues when PCs Seek Insight, as a way to begin and end with the fiction as you a…

689.title: Creating clues
689.excerpt: If a provocative, juicy clue just occurs to you, or something in Book II suggests a clue that resonates, then ask yourself whether it contradicts your prep or any established truths. If not, use it! Otherwise: start with the revelation , th…

690.title: Prepping clues
690.excerpt: As you prep for a session, try to identify any crucial revelations that the PCs need in order to accomplish their current goals or to make important decisions. For each revelation, create at least one clue, ideally two or three. The players…

691.title: 1) Lay the foundation
691.excerpt: - Ask questions - Build on what you've got - Exploit the setting guide

692.title: 2) Build up its story
692.breadcrumb: Discoveries › 1) Lay the foundation › 2) Build up its story
692.excerpt: - Look for connections - Ask yourself questions - Answer those questions - Create a timeline

693.title: 3) Sketch out the site's contents
693.breadcrumb: Discoveries › 1) Lay the foundation › 2) Build up its story › 3) Sketch out the site's contents
693.excerpt: - Populate it - Identify dangers and discoveries - Establish areas/rooms - Describe the environment - Arrange areas/rooms

694.title: 4)Write it up as much as you like
694.breadcrumb: Discoveries › 4)Write it up as much as you like
694.excerpt: - Create a map or visual (maybe) - Detail each area/room - Subdivide as needed - Make lists and tables - Make plans - Review and revise See Creating sites for the full process, and Running sites for guidance on running sites .

695.title: Encounters
695.excerpt: When the PCs cross paths with someone or something that isn't immediately hostile or dangerous, that's an encounter. This usually means people, but an encounter can be with beasts, spirits, constructs, or anything else the PCs can interact…

696.title: Building encounters
696.excerpt: Answer each of these questions, in whatever order makes sense. As the encounter evolves in your mind, feel free to go back and tweak or change earlier answers. - Who/what is the encounter with? - Where/when does it happen? - What are they d…

697.title: Running encounters
697.excerpt: Run encounters like any other part of the game. Follow the core loop . Frame the action with the encounter about to start, or just starting, or already underway. Describe the environment and portray your characters. Make a soft GM move, ask…

698.title: Opportunities
698.excerpt: An opportunity is anything that the PCs come across that might benefit them, or that they could take advantage of. It's a very broad category, including: - Useful or valuable stuff (coin, trade goods, tools, weapons, Surplus, etc.) - Expend…

699.title: Artifacts
699.excerpt: Artifacts are things laden with meaning, power, or value. They're objects that the PCs might take with them, or come back later to claim. Relics of the past, gems and finery, magic items—treasure, basically! Book II contains over a hundred…

701.title: Placing artifacts
701.excerpt: Put artifacts in the game where they make sense. That sealed tomb that the PCs are exploring probably has grave goods! The sorcerer that they're hunting probably has a few magic artifacts at her disposal. Put artifacts in the game, too, whe…

702.title: Identifying artifacts
702.excerpt: When the PCs find an artifact, describe it! Tell them what they've found, and what's obvious at a glance. If the artifact is merely interesting, useful, or valuable , then make that clear as part of your initial description or as soon as th…

703.title: Artifacts in play
703.excerpt: Some artifacts are simply interesting or valuable. But others have extraordinary properties or generate magical effects. When questions arise about an artifact (like how it works, what's involved with using it, what it can/can't do, etc.),…

704.title: Artifacts as threats
704.excerpt: Think about how NPCs react to artifacts . Are they intrigued? Freaked out? Jealous? Does word of it spread to other towns? If the way people react to an artifact becomes a source of trouble, then write it up as a threat , a MacGuffin ). Lik…

705.title: Selling artifacts
705.excerpt: If an artifact has a listed Value, then PCs can Trade & Barter to sell it as if it were a special item. If they know or suspect that it's magical, they can try to leverage that for a better price—or they might need to hide its magical natur…

706.title: Buying artifacts
706.excerpt: As a rule, specific and/or magical artifacts are unique items—the PCs can't just Trade & Barter to acquire them. If a PC wants to acquire such an artifact, they might: - Know Things about where such things can be found, or who might have on…

707.title: Creating artifacts
707.excerpt: See Appendix B: Artifact Creation for the full process. Appendix B includes a detailed procedure for artifact creation. Use it when none of the existing artifacts in Book II meet your needs, or when you have an idea for an artifact that you…

708.title: 1 Origin and theme
708.excerpt: Pick or roll for the artifact's origin, the people who made it or the power to whom it's most closely tied (the Stone Lords, the Fae, the Things Below, etc.). If you already know where the artifact came from, just pick. If a few different o…

709.title: 2 Nature
709.excerpt: Pick or roll for the artifact's nature: a mundane item, a strange material, the tether for a spirit or sentience, etc. Each result on the nature table points to another table, or set of tables, to determine its characteristics. Pick from or…

710.title: 3 Form
710.excerpt: If it's not already obvious, pick or roll for the artifact's size ( small , , , immobile ), tags (like fragile or crude ), and whether it's particularly hard to access or otherwise take advantage of. From there, pick from or roll on the wha…

711.title: 4 Synthesis
711.excerpt: Take the details you've established so far and imagine the artifact that they suggest. Fill in missing details and make decisions. What is this artifact, exactly? What was it originally for? How did it come to be, or be here ? You don't nee…

712.title: 5 Write-up
712.excerpt: Once you have a clear picture of the artifact in your mind, write it up as one of the following.

713.title: As a brief description
713.breadcrumb: 4 Synthesis › 5 Write-up › As a brief description
713.excerpt: Write something short and sweet, 30 words or less, including load, tags, and other game elements (see Tags). This approach is best for simple, non-magical artifacts, but it can also work for artifacts with a straightforward magical effect.…

714.title: As a detailed description
714.breadcrumb: 4 Synthesis › 5 Write-up › As a detailed description
714.excerpt: Give the artifact a title, either something descriptive ("a stone cup") or a proper name ("the Red Pennant"). Assign it tags, load (if any), and other game elements (see Tags). Then write a few sentences about it, what it looks like, what i…

715.title: As player moves
715.excerpt: Write a detailed description (as per the previous page). Then write a custom player move to reflect how the artifact works or what PCs can do with it. Custom moves are a great way to reflect artifacts with magical powers, especially ones th…

716.title: As a steading improvement
716.excerpt: If the artifact could benefit the village of Stonetop as a whole, then write it up as a steading improvement. This is a good way to reflect resources (like a mine or a water source), improvements to the food supply (a new crop, weather magi…

717.title: REDWOOD BRAZIER

718.title: As an arcanum
718.excerpt: If you envision the artifact providing a lasting or reusable benefit, but only after the PCs spend time and effort unlocking it, then consider writing it up as a minor arcanum . If you envision a magical artifact growing in power as a PC le…

719.title: Tags and game elements
719.excerpt: Regardless of how you're writing up an artifact, include a list of relevant gear terms & tags , such as: +1 armor, awkward , crude , dangerous , fragile , requires \ \ \ , slow , uses, etc. If it can be used as a weapon or a light source, i…

720.title: Arcana
720.excerpt: An arcanum is a card or playbook insert, something you give the players. Each one represents a discovery—an artifact, a place, a bit of lore, a magical effect, etc.— with mysteries that the PCs can unlock. "Arcanum" is a game term; it's not…

721.title: Placing arcana
721.excerpt: Be thoughtful about how often you introduce new arcana. Between unlocking them, using them, and dealing with their consequences, they can have a huge impact on the game. Give the players too many and they'll be overwhelmed with options. Giv…

723.title: Identifying arcana
723.excerpt: Be generous with info about arcana. You want the players to know what's involved in unlocking them and using them. They need that info to make informed decisions. That's why arcana are handouts; you're meant to give them to the players. Whe…

724.title: Unlocking arcana
724.excerpt: To unlock their mysteries, many arcana (especially minor arcana) ask the PCs to invest significant time and effort. They have requirements like, "spend a few weeks deciphering the glyphs," or, "acquire the special ingredients," or "risk las…

725.title: Consequences
725.excerpt: Each major arcanum has a list of potential consequences, with one or more checkboxes next to each one. Players mark a consequence when one of the arcanum's moves says to, or when you tell them to do so as a hard GM move. The player decides…

726.title: Sharing arcana
726.excerpt: PCs can share arcana with each other, or even with NPCs, as long as it makes sense to do so. If the PCs share a minor arcanum with each other, work with them to figure out how the requirements might change. The next winter, after a year of…

727.title: NPCs using artifacts and arcana
727.breadcrumb: 4 Synthesis › NPCs using artifacts and arcana
727.excerpt: At some point, an NPC or monster will get their hands on a magical artifact or arcanum. The PCs might give it to them, or teach them to use it or the spell it imparts. Or, the NPC might acquire/steal/borrow it from the PCs. Or it might be i…

728.title: Creating minor arcana
728.excerpt: Write up an artifact as a minor arcanum when… - … you can envision a meaningful set of steps for unlocking its mysteries - … once unlocked, it provides a lasting, repeatable, and/or renewable benefit, and… - … you think you can describe it…

729.title: Initial title
729.excerpt: Write a short, simple phrase describing what the arcanum appears to be. A minor arcanum card can fit 20 characters of 12-point type, so aim for that. Examples: "A set of clay tablets," or "A broken glass sword."

730.title: Tags and others elements
730.excerpt: List the arcanum's tags, load, and other game elements . See Tags for guidance.

731.title: Brief description
731.excerpt: What is it? How do the PCs encounter it and perceive it? How does it make them feel? Keep it short, like 75 words or less, but also make it as punchy as you can.

732.title: Requirements
732.excerpt: Write a brief intro, like "To unlock the mysteries of these tablets, you must…" Follow that with a series of checkbox steps. The requirements might involve… - … deciphering/studying something; - … learning something, or figuring it out; - ……

733.title: Unlocked title
733.excerpt: Write something grand and significantsounding, or at least something distinct. Write them in Title Case, as proper nouns. You can get 20 characters in one line, or 40 characters in two lines. Examples: "Ranaji Kav's Final Technique," or "Th…

734.title: Mysteries
734.excerpt: Write out the back of the minor arcanum, the custom move(s) and/or follower(s) gained by a PC who unlocks its mysteries. If the arcanum has different tags once it's unlocked, write them here. Try to reflect the origin, theme(s), and charact…

735.title: Creating major arcana
735.excerpt: A major arcanum is a half-page playbook insert . Write up a discovery as a major arcanum only if… - … you're willing to do a fair amount of work to create it; - … you're okay with it being a significant part of your ongoing game; - … you ca…

736.title: Title, tags, and description
736.breadcrumb: Creating major arcana › Title, tags, and description
736.excerpt: Give your major arcanum a Proper Title, something capitalized, evocative, maybe a little ominous. The "Misty Mirror" and "The Everflame" are good examples. List its tags and other game elements , including its load (if any). Write a brief d…

737.title: Starting moves
737.breadcrumb: Creating major arcana › Title, tags, and description › Starting moves
737.excerpt: Write the starting move(s), the ones that are easily accessible to a PC who acquires the arcanum. These should be useful and compelling, something the player(s) will be excited (or at least tempted) to use.

738.title: How it's unlocked
738.breadcrumb: Creating major arcana › Title, tags, and description › Starting moves › How it's unlocked
738.excerpt: Write the requirements as a distinct move, as a checklist, or as part of the arcanum's initial move(s). These requirements might involve… - … successfully using one of the arcanum's starting moves \ \ times; - … rolling a 6- on one of the i…

739.title: Mysteries
739.excerpt: Write the extra move(s), options, and/or followers that a PC can gain by unlocking the arcanum. They should be quite potent, but using them should require or at least risk marking consequences. If there are multiple mysteries to unlock, dec…

740.title: Consequences
740.excerpt: Create 5-10 consequences that a PC might incur when they use the arcanum's mysteries. If you write 6 or fewer, give some of them multiple checkboxes to indicate that they can be suffered multiple times. A major arcanum should have 8 checkbo…

741.title: Edit and revise
741.breadcrumb: Creating major arcana › Edit and revise
741.excerpt: Look at what you've got so far, and revise mercilessly. The arcanum's starting moves, its mysteries and the way they're unlocked, and its consequences should all reflect its concept and themes. They should suggest a story, but not dictate o…

742.title: NPCs & Followers

743.title: NPCs
743.excerpt: NPCs are non-player characters , anyone who exists in the game's fiction who isn't one of the PCs. This includes people, of course, but also includes spirits of the wild, shades of the departed, and bound entities. It can even include beast…

744.title: NPCs in play
744.excerpt: Part of your job as the GM is to portray NPCs. What they do, what they say, how they feel—that's all up to you! Practically, you portray NPCs as part of the core loop of play The core loop. As you establish a situation, say (or ask) what NP…

745.title: Modes of portrayal
745.excerpt: When you portray an NPC, you can speak in their voice , in first-person ("I… just don't know if I can keep… doing this"). Or, you can summarize what they say and how they say it, in third-person ("She tears up and says she can't keep doing…

746.title: Multiple NPCs at once
746.breadcrumb: NPCs & Followers › Multiple NPCs at once
746.excerpt: You'll inevitably have some scenes where multiple NPCs are in conflict or at least have conflicting opinions. In such scenes, you need to balance giving your NPCs life with being a fan of the PCs . Try to keep the conversation centered on t…

747.title: Dialogue scenes
747.excerpt: Many scenes will be dialogue scenes, scenes where one or more PCs are talking to an NPC, and that's the extent of the action. It's easy to fall into "just talking," where you all are inhabiting the PCs and NPCs and just play-acting, seeing…

748.title: Creating NPCs
748.excerpt: Remember that one of your principles is to give your characters life . You want your NPCs to be distinct and memorable, believable and compelling people instead of just names on a sheet. A world full of interesting NPCs is a rich and myster…

749.title: 1 Name
749.excerpt: Give the NPC a name, or ask the players to do so. Choose from the appropriate name list or Book II entry, or use a name with a similar sound or origin. - Stonetop names are Welsh-inspired - Marshedge names are Irish-inspired - Hillfolk name…

750.title: 2 Concept
750.excerpt: Figure out who this person is. What are the most obvious and important things about them? What are their pronouns? How old they are? Where are they from? If you had to describe them in just a few words, what would they be?

751.title: Build on what's established
751.excerpt: Start with what you already know about this NPC and extrapolate. If the NPC is written up as a follower, monster, or threat, then their concept is already well-established and you just need to fill in some details. But if you're making up t…

752.title: Ask questions
752.breadcrumb: 2 Concept › Build on what's established › Ask questions
752.excerpt: Many NPCs will be people who the PCs would know, or least know of, so ask your players about them. They'll care more about NPCs that they help create! Keep the Line in mind. Ask the characters questions that they'd be able to answer. The cl…

753.title: For locals or people PCs know well:
753.breadcrumb: For locals or people PCs know well:
753.excerpt: - Are you related to them? How? - What's their family situation? (Married? Kids? Parents? Siblings?) - Who else are they close to? Who cares about them? - What do you like/dislike about them? - What are they respected for? - What do others…

754.title: For outsiders, but ones the PCs know:
754.breadcrumb: For locals or people PCs know well: › For outsiders, but ones the PCs know:
754.excerpt: - When/how did you first meet them? - When did you last see them? - What do you know of their family? - How would you describe them? - Why do you (not) look forward to seeing them again? - How have they changed since last you met?

755.title: For folks the PCs have heard of but don't actually know:
755.breadcrumb: For folks the PCs have heard of but don't actually know:
755.excerpt: - What are they known for? - What is said to be their most notable trait? - Who do you know who's met them? - How are they different than what you expected? For specific NPCs, you can be much more provocative. Frame your questions so that t…

756.breadcrumb: For folks the PCs have heard of but don't actually know: › Assign memorable traits
756.excerpt: Give the NPC at least one specific, memorable trait and play that trait up. These traits become your touchstones for portraying them. Folks might not remember that the publican's daughter is named "Tegid," but they'll remember "the girl who…

757.title: 3 Impressions
757.breadcrumb: For folks the PCs have heard of but don't actually know: › 3 Impressions
757.excerpt: At this point, you should have a solid concept for the NPC, enough that you can start portraying them. If you're creating this NPC on the fly, frame a scene with them (if you haven't already) and give up to 3 impressions about them , their…

799.excerpt: Player moves—especially the homefront player moves —will introduce unexpected opportunities, challenges, and turns of events. Let those surprises illuminate the village and make it feel real. Life is full of surprises. Your version of Stone…

802.excerpt: Whenever you wonder something about the village or about daily life, consider asking the PCs about it. Ask what they're up to as you frame the action. "Caradoc, what task or chore are you working on?" Ask for details , to help paint the sce…

811.title: Bring it home
811.excerpt: Make the village the foundation of the game. Get the players to help create and define the village and the NPCs who live there. Give them a chance to care. Then threaten the village, offer opportunities to make things better, and show how t…

812.title: Let things breathe

812.excerpt: Your job is to punctuate the PCs lives with adventure . There should be stretches of time in which very little happens. Gloss over days, weeks, or months. Let the PCs work on long-term projects or just live their lives. Maybe a season passe…

814.title: Running the homefront

815.title: Before the first adventure
