831.excerpt: Have an NPC invite or prompt a PC to open up, to talk, to process what they're going through. Or have an NPC show them kindness or concern, and ask what they do. Invite the player to portray a compelling character , and give your characters…

832.title: <sup>ä</sup> Change a relationship
832.breadcrumb: Let things burn › <sup>ä</sup> Change a relationship
832.excerpt: A loved one grows distant. A friendship is lost. A peer becomes a rival. A couple calls it quits. Etc. The relationships involved can be PC-to-NPC or between NPCs. The change can be big and dramatic, or subtle, or anywhere in between. If th…

833.title: <sup>ä</sup> Oppose their wishes
833.breadcrumb: Let things burn › <sup>ä</sup> Oppose their wishes
833.excerpt: Have NPCs object to the PCs' plans, or get in the way, or need to be convinced. Or have them do things on their own, something the PCs don't want them to do, and see how the PCs respond. As a hard move, have someone with actual clout put th…

834.title: <sup>ä</sup> Remind them of their obligations
834.breadcrumb: Let things burn › <sup>ä</sup> Remind them of their obligations
834.excerpt: Point out something that needs doing or that is expected of them. Have an NPC make explicit demands on their time. Warn them of what might happen if they ignore their responsibilities. Show them the consequences if they do. Use this move to…

835.title: <sup>ä</sup> Start a conflict or crisis
835.breadcrumb: Let things burn › <sup>ä</sup> Start a conflict or crisis
835.excerpt: Bring simmering tensions to a boil. Have two NPCs start screaming at each other or come to blows. Have someone call out a PC, loudly and in public. Have panic spread. Or: have a house catch on fire, a monster attack the village, a dead body…

836.title: Use other moves, too!
836.breadcrumb: Let things burn › Use other moves, too!
836.excerpt: Just to be clear, you can and should use your standard GM moves during homefront play. Announce trouble ("Isn't it odd how Iwan is, like, charming and has friends now?"), ask provocative questions ("Who's going to be most opposed to this pl…

837.title: The steading playbook
837.breadcrumb: Let things burn › The steading playbook
837.excerpt: The village of Stonetop gets its own playbook, reproduced starting on The Steading. You'll use this document to track Stonetop's: - Stats and tags - Resources and fortifications - Current debilities - Improvements (and progress towards them…

838.title: Other steadings
838.excerpt: Other steadings have stats and details similar to Stonetop's, which you can find in Book II . Use those stats should the PCs end up Trading & Bartering in another steading, Mustering or Deploying its people, or having them Pull Together. Wi…

839.title: Fortunes
839.excerpt: The Fortunes stat reflects the morale and social cohesion of the village, the favor of the gods, and just "how things are going." Fortunes start at +1, and can range from -1 to +3. When Fortunes are high, people are (generally) happy and ge…

840.title: Surplus
840.excerpt: Surplus reflects stores of food and trade goods. It represents crops in the granary and in household larders, livestock ready for slaughter, whisky that's ready to drink, fur that's ready to trade or be fashioned into garments. Surplus isn'…

841.title: Size
841.excerpt: Each steading has a Size tag, which describes the scale of its infrastructure: its permanent buildings, the size of its fields, the number of inhabitants it can comfortably support. The tag is mostly descriptive, but it affects how much Sur…

842.title: Population
842.excerpt: The steading's Population reflects the number of able bodies living there, relative to its Size. Population starts play at +0, and can range from -1 to +3. When Population is -1, buildings stand empty and key jobs go undone. Labor and free…

843.title: Prosperity
843.excerpt: Prosperity represents the goods that circulate in the steading, the number and variety of tradesfolk, and how often merchants pass through town. While Surplus represents how much food and trade goods are in the town, Prosperity reflects the…

844.title: Resources
844.excerpt: A steading's Prosperity is closely tied to its resources . On the steading playbook, these are listed to the right of the current Prosperity. At the start of play, Stonetop's resources include: - Farming (beans, potatoes, oats, and barley)…

845.title: Defenses
845.excerpt: Defenses represent the martial prowess and readiness of the steading: how well-trained and well-armed the residents are, how many veteran warriors live there, how wellorganized they are. Refer to a steading's Defenses to help decide how qui…

846.title: Fortifications
846.excerpt: Each steading has a list of fortifications and other resources that contribute to its Defenses. On the steading playbook, these are listed to the right of the current Defenses. At the start of play, Stonetop's fortifications (etc.) include:…

847.title: Debilities
847.excerpt: During play, the steading can suffer debilities much like a PC can Debilities. There are three steading debilities: - Diminished , by injury, sickness, doubt. Players have disadvantage to Deploy, Muster, or Pull Together - Lacking , due to…

848.title: Steading improvements
848.excerpt: The steading playbook includes a number of potential improvements that can be unlocked in play. Each has a list of requirements and benefits that the steading gains upon completion.

849.title: Requirements
849.excerpt: The requirements for each improvement vary from clear and obvious ("Impressing a band of Hillfolk") to the seemingly impossible ("Some method of making water flow uphill"). If the players don't know how to go about meeting such a requiremen…

850.title: Benefits
850.excerpt: Once the PCs meet all the requirements for an improvement, the steading gains the listed benefit(s). Many improvements include a benefit of "increase Fortunes by 1." This is a one-time gain; when Seasons Change and Fortunes reset Seasons Ch…

851.title: Losing improvements
851.excerpt: Once an improvement is completed, assume that basic upkeep is done by the community as a whole. For example, once the Palisade is built, assume that the steading keeps it in good repair. Some improvements have additional, ongoing costs, lik…

852.title: Other improvements
852.excerpt: The improvements listed in the steading playbook aren't the only ones possible. You can find others in various entries of Book II , revealing them to the players as appropriate. Likewise, you might have an idea for an additional improvement…

853.title: Assets
853.excerpt: The steading playbook includes a section for recording assets that are owned by the residents of Stonetop in common. At the start of play, this includes: - A pair of hardy draft horses - A pair of horse-drawn plows (iron) - A pair of carts…

854.title: Residents & neighbors
854.excerpt: The last two pages of the steading playbook are for tracking NPCs. Whenever you or a player introduces a new NPC, make note of them here: their name, pronouns, profession, and any notable traits and relationships. See Creating NPCs for guid…

855.title: Seasons Change
855.excerpt: The Seasons Change move creates the long-term rhythm of the game. It breaks up downtime. It requires the PCs to care about the steading's Fortunes and Surplus . It makes many of the steading improvements important and valuable to pursue.

856.title: Making the move
856.excerpt: The Seasons Change during downtime. When you've played out any activities, scenes, and situations you and the players are interested in, skip ahead in time and re-solve this move. Aim to resolve the move towards the end of a session if you…

857.title: Rolling
857.excerpt: Each season indicates who rolls for the move (whoever is most hopeful rolls in spring, whoever is most content rolls in summer, etc.). "Whoever" refers to the PC, not the player. If you really can't decide, pick randomly. Whoever rolls also…

858.title: Generating Surplus
858.breadcrumb: Making the move › Rolling › Generating Surplus
858.excerpt: In summer, the village generates 1d4-1 Surplus (from pickled spring vegetables, surplus winter potatoes, summer fruits, whisky maturing, game, etc.). If they also get a seasonal gain, the player can wait to pick it until they see how much S…

859.title: Improving the rolls
859.breadcrumb: Making the move › Rolling › Improving the rolls
859.excerpt: PCs cannot Aid each other on either the +Fortunes roll or on rolls to generate Surplus. The move assumes that everyone is doing their part already. PCs can Bolster for a coming season and spend the Preparation on the roll(s), but doing so s…

860.title: SEASONS CHANGE
860.excerpt: When spring bursts forth upon the land , whoever is the most hopeful rolls +Fortunes: on a 10+ , pick 1 seasonal gain; on a 7-9 , pick 1 seasonal gain, but a threat to the steading makes itself known or gets worse; on a 6- , threats abound…

861.title: Winter
861.excerpt: In winter, the village consumes 1d4 + Population Surplus. If Stonetop has shrunk to a hamlet , it consumes only 1d2 + Population. If it has grown to a town , it consumes 2d6 + Population. If there's not enough, then Surplus drops to 0, the…

862.title: Seasonal gains
862.excerpt: On a 7+ in spring or autumn or a 7-9 in summer, the rolling player gets to pick a seasonal gain. On a 10+ in summer, they get to pick two. The choices are: - Population boom: A number of youth come of age, and/or outsiders settle here. Incr…

863.title: "A threat to the steading…"
863.breadcrumb: Making the move › "A threat to the steading…"
863.excerpt: On a 7-9 in spring or autumn, or a 6- in summer, either introduce a new threat to the steading or have an existing threat advance towards impending doom . The threat you pick can be internal or external, imminent or distant, uncaring or act…

864.title: Threats abound
864.excerpt: On a 6- in spring, autumn, or winter, "threats abound." Make things bad. A monster attacks the village, with little or no warning. Multiple threats advance towards impending doom . Simmering tensions boil over. The weather is bad, crops fai…

865.title: Putting the results in play
865.breadcrumb: Making the move › Putting the results in play
865.excerpt: Pick threats and opportunities based on the results of the Seasons Change move, the established fiction, the needs of your game, and the wants of your group. As you make your decisions, consider: - Are there any threats barreling towards th…

866.title: SEASONS CHANGE EXAMPLES
866.breadcrumb: Making the move › SEASONS CHANGE EXAMPLES
866.excerpt: It's the end of our first session, and we've just finished making and introducing characters. The group kept focusing on the recent crinwin raids, the abducted children, and the effect this has had on the village. The village starts with 1…

867.title: Player moves
867.excerpt: These moves become relevant during homefront play, or at least when the PCs are dealing with lots of folks from home. Some might also apply when the PCs travel to another community.

868.title: BOLSTER
868.excerpt: When you prepare for what's coming or seek the favor of the gods , say how and answer the GM's questions. Then, hold Preparation based on the amount of time you devote: - A week or so: 1 Preparation - A month or so: 2 Preparation - The bett…

869.title: CONVALESCE
869.excerpt: When you rest for a few days, in safety and comfort , recover all your HP and clear all your debilities. When you rest for a few weeks under the care of a healer , heal any problematic wounds that can heal. If you have suffered a permanent…

870.title: DEPLOY
870.excerpt: When you send a steading's people into danger or rally them against an attack , roll +Defenses: on a 10+ , it goes as well as can be expected; on a 7-9 , it works but someone picks 1 from the list below. If the steading is acting from a pos…

871.title: LEVEL UP
871.excerpt: When you have a quiet stretch of time at home and XP equal to (or greater than) 6 + twice your current level , follow these steps: - 1) Subtract 6 + twice your current level from your XP. - 2) Increase your level by 1. - 3) Choose a new mov…

872.title: MAKE A PLAN
872.breadcrumb: Making the move › MAKE A PLAN
872.excerpt: When you wish to accomplish some project but don't know how to go about it , tell the GM what you hope to achieve. They'll say what's required. If you're stumped on how to accomplish one of the requirements, tell the GM and Make a Plan for…

873.title: MEET WITH DISASTER
873.breadcrumb: Making the move › MEET WITH DISASTER
873.excerpt: When calamity befalls the steading or panic spreads , reduce Fortunes by 1 (min -1). When Fortunes would drop below -1 for any reason (not just calamity or panic), then the GM picks 1 instead: - The steading marks diminished from injuries/s…

874.title: MUSTER
874.excerpt: When you press every able body into the defense of a steading , reduce Fortunes by 1 and roll +Population: on a 7+ , the steading is alert and ready for action until the threat passes, the Seasons Change, or you cease to oversee the muster.…

875.title: PULL TOGETHER
875.excerpt: When you set a community to work on improvements , to secure new resources, or to make major repairs, spend whatever the GM says is required (time, materiel, Surplus, etc.) and roll +Population: on a 10+ , the job gets done; on a 7-9 , pick…

876.title: TRADE & BARTER
876.excerpt: When you wish to acquire or sell a commonly available item , you can. When you seek to acquire or sell a special item , roll +Prosperity and subtract the item's Value. In winter, you have disadvantage. On a 10+ , you can get it or sell it f…

877.breadcrumb: Making the move › On a 7-9 when the PC is looking to sell,
877.excerpt: present an NPC who's only willing to give something of lower Value. Like, if the PC is selling a Value 2 item, they might offer a few Value 1 items—or even just one such item! Maybe they don't really want what the PC is selling, or maybe th…

878.title: On a 7-9 when the PC is looking to buy...
878.breadcrumb: Making the move › On a 7-9 when the PC is looking to buy...
878.excerpt: - ... picking "it'll cost more than usual" means that the NPC wants something of a higher Value, or a really good exchange rate, or an added favor, debt, or service. Establish what they're asking, and make it clear that the NPC is willing t…

879.title: Value and coins
879.breadcrumb: Making the move › Value and coins
879.excerpt: The world of Stonetop has no central government, no common currency, and no standard exchange rates. So, what constitutes a fair trade? Any given item has a Value from 0 to 4 (or rarely higher). Values are not linear; they are tiers. A sing…

880.title: A Value 0 item is generally worth:
880.breadcrumb: A Value 0 item is generally worth:
880.excerpt: - A purse of copper coins - A single silver coin - A favor - A few days of unskilled labor - A common, mundane item

881.title: A Value 1 item is generally worth:
881.breadcrumb: A Value 0 item is generally worth: › A Value 1 item is generally worth:
881.excerpt: - A handful of silver coins - A season (or so) of unskilled labor - A few days of skilled labor - A unit of trade goods\ (a sack of grain, a pouch of salt, a stack of pelts, etc.) - A bit of finery (a richly embroidered cloak, a silk scarf,…

882.title: A Value 2 item is generally worth:
882.breadcrumb: A Value 0 item is generally worth: › A Value 2 item is generally worth:
882.excerpt: - A purse of silver coins - A single gold coin - A Surplus Surplus - A year (or so) of unskilled labor - A season (or so) of skilled labor - A cartload of common trade goods\ - An item of luxury or status (a gold ring, an artful silver torc…

883.title: A Value 3 item is generally worth:
883.breadcrumb: A Value 0 item is generally worth: › A Value 3 item is generally worth:
883.excerpt: - A handful of gold coins - A year (or so) of skilled labor - A good, trained horse or mule - A precious item (ruby ring, gold torc, etc.)

884.title: A Value 4 item is generally worth:
884.breadcrumb: A Value 0 item is generally worth: › A Value 4 item is generally worth:
884.excerpt: - A purse of gold coins - A dozen or so good horses - A "priceless" item (huge flawless gemstone, a gold statuette, a bejeweled scepter, etc.) \ Exotic trade goods are +1 Value A purse of coins contains about 10 handfuls. A handful of coins…

885.title: What to prep
885.breadcrumb: A Value 0 item is generally worth: › What to prep
885.excerpt: If you know that an upcoming session will involve the homefront, there's plenty that you can prepare in advance. If the PCs are heading home , review the Aftermath section Aftermath. Take some time to think through: - What's happened while…

886.excerpt: - Keep Company on way home? - En route: traps & snares sprung, game gone uncollected - Anti-crinwin charms strung up by the Stream, swaying in breeze - Folks trudging in from the fields and gardens; somber & on-edge

887.title: Return Triumphant

887.excerpt: - Excited cry as PCs are spotted - Crowd gathers in celebration - Tearful reunions: Rheinal hugs Pryder, Glenys scoops up Wynfor - Wynfor's weird, unfocused gaze - Rheinal toasts the PCs; cheers! - Morwenna embraces Caradoc, happy, proud; s…

888.title: Put them in a spot
888.breadcrumb: A Value 0 item is generally worth: › Put them in a spot
888.excerpt: - Glenys: demands to know what's wrong with Wynfor - Owain blames Rhianna for his daughter's condition ('none of this would've happened if you weren't a coward last winter!')

889.title: 'Family' scenes

889.excerpt: - Rhianna celebrating with her crew; bit of a crowd gathered; Garet talks up the PCs, talks smack about Owain - Blodwen with Seren & Gwendyl: debrief, discuss what's to be done about the kids - Caradoc with his grandma; a rare moment of luc…

890.title: Potential GM moves
890.breadcrumb: A Value 0 item is generally worth: › Potential GM moves
890.excerpt: - Offer an opportunity: Vahid has time to talk to Mindgem; get info dump about Green Lords, etc. - Remind them of their obligations: Sawyl is proud of Caradoc, but there's work to do, boy! - Play them against each other: Eira & Lowri talk a…

891.title: Writing Moves & Love Letters
891.excerpt: Writing moves Writing love letters Player moves are little blocks of rules. Each move kicks in at certain times and says what happens. Between "standard" moves like Defy Danger and Seasons Change, playbook moves, arcana, and all the custom…

892.title: Writing moves
892.breadcrumb: Writing Moves & Love Letters › Writing moves
892.excerpt: To write a new player-facing move: - 1) Take stock - What does the move reflect/model? - What constraints do you have? - What are your goals for the move? - Do you really need the move? - 2) Draft the trigger(s) - When does the move kick in…

893.title: Naming moves
893.breadcrumb: Writing Moves & Love Letters › Naming moves
893.excerpt: Give your move a name if… - ... it's an option in a playbook, an insert, or a major arcanum; - ... you want other moves/rules to reference it (e.g. "When you Haggle with Grimstew …"); - ... you just want to name it. A move might even start…

894.title: 1 Take stock
894.excerpt: Think through the following. Take notes as you go, at least the first few times you do this.

895.title: What does the move reflect or model?
895.breadcrumb: 1 Take stock › What does the move reflect or model?
895.excerpt: Think about what the move represents. In what circumstance does it apply? What questions does it (help) answer? What sort of conflict does it resolve? What fictional situation, trait, or ability does it model via rules? For example: - "This…

896.title: What constraints do you have?
896.breadcrumb: 1 Take stock › What constraints do you have?
896.excerpt: What practical concerns and limitations do you need to work within? Does this move need to fit in a limited space? What other PC moves might interact with it? How much time are you willing to spend on this move in play? How much time do you…

897.title: What are your goals?
897.breadcrumb: 1 Take stock › What constraints do you have? › What are your goals?
897.excerpt: Think about what you want this move to add to the game. For example, your new move might… - ... grant permission, like how Spirit Tongue lets the Blessed talk to spirits and natural beasts. - ... establish how stuff works, like how the " at…

898.title: Do you really need this move?
898.breadcrumb: 1 Take stock › Do you really need this move?
898.excerpt: Whatever you intend your new move to reflect or model, how would you handle it in play without this new move? Would it even be possible? What questions might you ask the PCs? What GM moves might you make? How might the PCs respond? What exi…

899.title: Do you need multiple triggers? A move might need multiple triggers, each with its own result(s), if…
899.breadcrumb: 1 Take stock › Do you need multiple triggers? A move might need multiple triggers, each with its own result(s), if…
899.excerpt: - ... it covers multiple related but distinct activities, like how Recover has one trigger for restoring HP and another for treating debilities/wounds. - ... it's a playbook move that grants two or more related benefits, like how Catlike al…

900.title: Do the triggers need limits?
900.breadcrumb: 1 Take stock › Do the triggers need limits?
900.excerpt: Is your trigger too broad? If read literally, will it apply even when you don't intend it to? Revise the trigger to avoid such problems. You might do this by… - ... using more-specific language. The original trigger for Clash was, "When you…

901.title: 3 Brainstorm results
901.excerpt: Think about what happens when the move's trigger is met. What definitely happens? What might happen? If the move has multiple triggers, then think about what could happen as a result of each. A move's results can be… - ... fictional , where…

902.title: Definitely
902.excerpt: - Access the Fae paths - Hallucinate/see spirits

903.title: Maybe (good!)
903.excerpt: • Feel a profound connection to each other/the world/themselves

904.title: Maybe (bad!)
904.excerpt: - Need a bigger dose - Mark a debility - Freak out, have a bad trip - Draw attention from hostile spirits (maybe the shadowy presence!)

905.title: 4 Decide how you'll pick between results
905.breadcrumb: 4 Decide how you'll pick between results
905.excerpt: If you've identified multiple results that your move could have, then how will you decide between those potential results?

906.title: Have the player roll
906.breadcrumb: 4 Decide how you'll pick between results › Have the player roll
906.excerpt: Use a roll to reflect uncertainty or risk, or if you want there to be multiple tiers of success. The result of the roll can dictate the results directly, as with Know Things . Or it might affect the choices that get made, like how Seek Insi…

907.title: Have the player pick
907.breadcrumb: 4 Decide how you'll pick between results › Have the player pick
907.excerpt: When there are multiple equally attractive (or repellant) options, have the player pick between them. For example, when a player gets a 10+ to Clash , they pick between striking hard or avoiding their foe's attack. When they Forage , they d…

908.title: Have the target pick
908.breadcrumb: 4 Decide how you'll pick between results › Have the player pick › Have the target pick
908.excerpt: When a move forces another character to feel or react in a particular way, then the move should have that character's player (the GM for NPCs) pick between options. This maintains a player's agency over their PC, and allows the GM to mainta…

909.title: Have the GM pick
909.breadcrumb: 4 Decide how you'll pick between results › Have the player pick › Have the target pick › Have the GM pick
909.excerpt: Use this option sparingly. Reserve it for moves where you're in the best position to choose, based on your agenda, principles, prep, and knowledge of the situation—or when you want to emphasize a PC's lack of control. For example, because D…

910.title: Decide by procedure
910.breadcrumb: 4 Decide how you'll pick between results › Have the player pick › Decide by procedure
910.excerpt: Your move can have different results based on the fiction, like how Take the Measure lets the Marshal ask an extra question if the target fears or respects them. Or your move can have results based on mechanical interactions, like how Meet…

911.title: Decide by consensus
911.breadcrumb: 4 Decide how you'll pick between results › Have the player pick › Decide by consensus
911.excerpt: A few moves call on the group as a whole to choose an outcome, like how End of Session has everyone answer the XP questions together. This approach produces a "writer's room" effect, where the players talk about the game at a meta-level. It…

912.title: Mix and match
912.breadcrumb: 4 Decide how you'll pick between results › Have the player pick › Mix and match
912.excerpt: Many moves combine these approaches. Clash has the player roll to see if their maneuver works, and has them make a choice on a 10+. Invoke the Sun God has the player pick a consequence on a 7+, but the GM picks a second consequence on a 7-9…

913.title: Do something else
913.breadcrumb: 4 Decide how you'll pick between results › Do something else
913.excerpt: You can probably come up with variations on these methods, or entirely new ways to choose between the potential results. For my "take shrooms near a waystone" move, I've identified a potential good result (feeling a profound sense of connec…

914.title: 5 If there's a roll, decide how it works
914.breadcrumb: 4 Decide how you'll pick between results › 5 If there's a roll, decide how it works
914.excerpt: The standard structure for a move with a roll is: "When you trigger the move , roll 2d6 + STAT: on a 10+ , you get the best result; on a 7-9 , you get a result that's not as good." But you can modify this structure to meet the needs of your…

915.title: Should you define a 6- result?
915.breadcrumb: 4 Decide how you'll pick between results › Should you define a 6- result?
915.excerpt: Most moves don't say what happens on a 6-, which means that the player marks XP and you make a hard GM move—one that flows from the established fiction and that follows your principles. Write a 6- result for your move only when you have a g…

916.title: Are there any other modifiers?
916.breadcrumb: 4 Decide how you'll pick between results › Are there any other modifiers?
916.excerpt: Consider whether any modifiers should be baked into the move, to reflect something important about how the move works. For example, Trade & Barter (page 540) has players subtract the item's Value to reflect availability, and has them roll w…

917.title: What does each tier look like?
917.breadcrumb: 4 Decide how you'll pick between results › What does each tier look like?
917.excerpt: Typically, a 10+ is a clean success, a 7-9 is a limited or costly success, and 6- is bad news. But your move might… - ... still have a downside on a 10+, like how Invoke the Sun God still has the player pick a consequence as a way to limit…

918.title: Alternative roll structures
918.breadcrumb: 4 Decide how you'll pick between results › Alternative roll structures
918.excerpt: A few moves use a totally different type of roll, and yours can too. For example: - Wishing Candles has the player roll a Die of Fate to see what their wish costs them, emphasizing the candles' fickle nature. - Fear of the Flame uses a risk…

919.title: Okay, here's my full draft:
919.breadcrumb: 4 Decide how you'll pick between results › Okay, here's my full draft:
919.excerpt: When you take shrooms together near a waystone, under a full moon, roll +WIS. If you've taken shrooms before, roll with advantage. On a 10+, you experience a profound sense of connectedness to your companions and the world; on a 7-9, pick 1…

920.title: Hold and spend
920.breadcrumb: 4 Decide how you'll pick between results › Hold and spend
920.excerpt: Some moves have the player "hold x currency," where the currency is something specific like "Readiness" or "Nerve." The move then says how the player can spend that currency, and what happens when they do. That could be a mechanical bonus (…

921.title: Imagine it in play
921.breadcrumb: 4 Decide how you'll pick between results › Hold and spend › Imagine it in play
921.excerpt: Envision a situation where this move could come up in play. Picture your players triggering it. Run through the move. Try to read it with fresh eyes. Imagine your players' reactions. If anything strikes you as unclear, confusing, or awkward…

922.title: Make sure it meets your goals
922.breadcrumb: 4 Decide how you'll pick between results › Make sure it meets your goals
922.excerpt: Think back on what you intended this move to do (see What are your goals?). Is it doing that? Is it doing it better than what you'd get if you just relied on the core loop, your GM moves, and the player moves already in play? If not, why no…

923.title: Check for common pitfalls
923.excerpt: Is the trigger too broad or too specific, such that the move will trigger more often or less often than you want? If so, fix it. Does your trigger confound outcome with intent? Have you written "When you solve the puzzle " instead of "When…

924.title: Punch it up
924.excerpt: Look for ways to make the move grabbier, more exciting, more thematic. Formidable doesn't trigger "when a battle starts." It triggers "When you wade into battle ." When the Lightbearer gets a 10+ on Guiding Light , "you all make it through…

925.title: Edit for length and clarity
925.breadcrumb: Punch it up › Edit for length and clarity
925.excerpt: Cut any word that's unneeded. Read each sentence, look for a cleaner way to phrase it. Reduce the move to its simplest version, while maintaining clarity and impact.

926.title: Check grammar, formatting
926.breadcrumb: Punch it up › Check grammar, formatting
926.excerpt: Make sure you're using the right words, that your subjects and verbs agree, that your bullet lists use parallel language. Check your spelling. If typing them out, put triggers in bold and italic text . Put thresholds in bold, like, " on a 1…

927.title: Get feedback
927.breadcrumb: Punch it up › Check grammar, formatting › Get feedback
927.excerpt: Ask others for feedback. Share your move online, with other Stonetop players and GMs. Share it with your players, too (unless you want it to be a surprise). Call out specific areas where you'd like input, but be open to any feedback you get…

928.title: More examples
928.excerpt: Here's another example of a situational move, this one written to reflect the party's current, sour mood. (That last bullet was originally "What do you do that I find so annoying?" But a fellow GM suggested this replacement. Hooray for feed…

929.title: MISERY LOVES COMPANY
929.excerpt: When you Keep Company while discord plagues the party , take turns asking the following questions instead: - What duty are you/we neglecting? - What's eating at you, and how do you take it out on others? - How do I make things worse? - What…

930.title: SCOUT AHEAD
930.excerpt: When you go off on your own into a dangerous place , the GM can have you Scout Ahead. If they do, say how you do it and roll +STAT (per Defy Danger). On a 7+, you make it back and the GM says where you got to and what you learned. Then, on…

931.title: BURGLE
931.excerpt: When you Scout Ahead , you can pick 1 extra option (even on a 6-, pick 1), and add this to your list of options: • You swiped something useful and/or valuable

932.title: Writing love letters
932.excerpt: Love letters are like single-use custom moves, addressed to a specific PC. Write them as part of your prep and hand them out at the start of a session (or whenever it makes sense). They're especially useful for: - Glossing over downtime - R…

933.title: Some examples:
933.excerpt: Dear Rhianna, You got back from a few days hunting to find everyone talking about Caradoc, Vahid, and their trip to the Tower. As if you didn't have enough to worry about. Speaking of worries, roll +Fortunes: on a 10+, pick 1; on a 7-9, pic…

934.title: Dear Caradoc,
934.excerpt: You and Morwenna are still tied up, but you're at the greenhouse. Tell us what unpleasant truth you had to reveal to seal the deal, then roll +CHA: on a 10+, pick 2; on a 7-9, pick 1; on a 6-, pick 1, mark XP, and your captors clearly know…

935.title: The Game Ongoing

936.title: Ending a session
936.breadcrumb: The Game Ongoing › Ending a session

937.title: Do the move
937.breadcrumb: The Game Ongoing › Do the move
937.excerpt: Make time for the End of Session move (at right) each time you play. Start to wrap up the action 30 minutes before folks need to leave, and give yourselves at least 15 minutes to do the End of Session move. Set a timer. It's important. Take…

938.title: END OF SESSION
938.breadcrumb: The Game Ongoing › END OF SESSION
938.excerpt: When a session ends , point out how you demonstrated or struggled with your instinct. If you can, mark XP. Say how your relationship with or opinion of a PC, NPC, or group has changed. If you can, mark XP. Answer these questions as a group.…

939.title: Handle logistics
939.excerpt: Schedule the next session if you can. If not, pick a tentative date or two and make plans to confirm. Games die more often from scheduling neglect than from lack of interest. Identify follow-up tasks. Who needs to do what between now and ne…

940.title: Take notes
940.excerpt: What happened this session that you don't want to forget? What are your next steps? Write that stuff down. Now. While it's still fresh in your mind. You don't need to write a full session recap right now (it's late, you're probably tired).…

941.title: Between sessions
941.excerpt: Use the time between sessions to tend to the game: to reflect, take notes, prep, and make plans.

942.title: Reflection
942.excerpt: Remember: GMing is a practice . You get better at it by doing it, and by trying to do better next time. Spend time after each session reflecting on the previous one. Ask yourself: - What goals had you set for yourself? Did you meet them? If…

943.title: Session recap(s)
943.excerpt: Every session should get a written recap, an official history of what happened. Few people can recall the events of a session a few days later, much less after weeks or months. So make sure the key moments of each session get written down.…

944.title: Caradoc and Vahid:
944.breadcrumb: Between sessions › Session recap(s) › Caradoc and Vahid:
944.excerpt: - Went to Ruined Tower - Ghost stories at the Crossroads! - Explored outer ruins, got makerglass shard and bronze plaque w/spell - Beznpol attacked; took its hide

945.title: Blodwen:
945.excerpt: - Big public fight with Tegwen, renounces her as kin - Moves out, moves in with Seren - Tegwen is petty & mean (surprise!) - Lewela visits, tearful support Rhianna (not present, off hunting?) Do whatever works for your table, but make sure…

946.title: Bookkeeping
946.excerpt: Pull out your notes, the steading playbook, your threat tracker—whatever tools you're using—and bring them up to date. - 1) Update your threats , following the procedure on Updating. - 2) Update your NPCs , wherever you track them. Cross of…

947.title: Follow-ups
947.excerpt: As the session ended, you likely found yourself with some follow-up tasks. As you reflect and take care of bookkeeping, you'll likely identify more. Review your to-do list and prioritize: - Start with time-sensitive tasks, stuff that needs…

948.title: Prep
948.excerpt: As the next session approaches, make time to sit down and prep. Think about where things left off last session. Where are the PCs and what are they up to? What do you expect to happen in the next session? Prep for that. If the PCs are… - ……

949.title: Other things you might prep
949.breadcrumb: Between sessions › Other things you might prep
949.excerpt: - An adventure hook to kick off a new adventure Wishes and personal goals - Custom moves to use in a specific situation. - Love letters to use at the start of a session. - Setup questions like those you asked at the start of the First Adven…

950.title: Adventure hooks
950.excerpt: If the last session ended in downtime, you might want to kick off a new adventure. Maybe the Seasons Changed and you owe the players an opportunity or threat. Maybe they got a 7-9 to Pull Together and choose "unforeseen cost, requirement, o…

951.title: Wishes and personal goals
951.breadcrumb: Between sessions › Wishes and personal goals
951.excerpt: Look over the wishes that your players gave you at the end of the last session, or any unmet wishes from before that. Look over the goals you wrote down as part of your reflection , or write 1-3 goals for yourself now—things you'd like to w…

952.title: Don't over-prep!
952.excerpt: Remember why you prep: to give yourself cool stuff to say, to speed up play, to shore up weaknesses, to hone your craft, and to give yourself permission to play hard and inflict real consequences. With that in mind, prep only: - 1) What you…

953.title: Check-ins
953.excerpt: Play with any group long enough, and you're bound to have issues. Players who get on each other's nerves. Mismatched expectations. Disruptive behaviors. Hurt feelings. Miscommunications. Etc. When there's a player-level issue, talk to them…

954.title: Starting a session
954.excerpt: Folks arrive at a session carrying their week with them—work, kids, traffic, etc. Give everyone a chance to settle. Chat and catch up. Let the noise of the world fade a bit before you start to play. Have a checklist of things to do at the s…

955.title: Logistics
955.excerpt: What are your plans for food and drink? Who's taking notes? When do folks need to leave? When should you take breaks? Etc. Set timers as needed. Be sure to save time for the End of Session move!

956.title: Recap
956.excerpt: Remind everyone what happened last time, and where things left off. If possible, get the players to do this themselves. Ask the notetaker to give a recap, or ask players to take turns doing so. Add details and make corrections as needed, bu…

957.title: Content guidelines
957.excerpt: As a group, read the "Content" section on your steading playbook. If you think this session might bump against these guidelines, say so now. Ask if anyone wants to add or change something. Remind folks to call "time out" if a guideline is b…

958.title: Highlighting
958.breadcrumb: Starting a session › Content guidelines › Highlighting
958.excerpt: Go over stuff that you want everyone to keep in mind or at least be aware of. This might include: - PC and/or follower instincts - Relationship changes from the end of last session - Outstanding wishes - Goals you're working on, that you'd…

959.title: Bookkeeping
959.breadcrumb: Starting a session › Content guidelines › Highlighting › Bookkeeping
959.excerpt: Have players update their playbooks and inserts if needed. Like, if they've had a few days at home since the last session, have them reset HP and clear debilities. If someone Leveled Up, make sure they've updated their XP and marked a new m…

960.title: Starting moves, questions, love letters
960.breadcrumb: Starting a session › Starting moves, questions, love letters
960.excerpt: If any PCs have moves that trigger at the start of session (like the Would-be Hero's "Destined" background), or if you've written setup questions or love letters, do them now. Let them serve as a final shift into actual play.

961.title: Opening scene(s)
961.excerpt: Address one or more PCs. Say (or ask) where they are, who they're with, what they're up to. Describe the environment, ask some questions. Portray NPCs. Make a soft GM move, something that propels the PC into action or conflict. Ask, "What d…

962.title: Pacing
962.excerpt: Stonetop tends to be slower-paced than many RPGs. It has its moments of drama and intense action. But it also encourages you to dwell on the details of daily life and to spend time chewing the scenery. Finding the right pace and rhythm for…

963.title: Adventures vs. homefront
963.breadcrumb: Starting a session › Adventures vs. homefront
963.excerpt: A key tension in the game is how much time to spend on adventures (expeditions and crises) vs. homefront play. The game won't always tell you when it's time to switch, and that's intentional. Use your players' wishes from the End of Session…

964.title: Increasing the pace
964.breadcrumb: Starting a session › Increasing the pace
964.excerpt: If you're not getting as much done in a session as you and your players would like, then try some of these: Skip the familiar. If you've played out trips on the West Road before, you don't need another montage. Say, "Four days later, you se…

965.title: Advancement
965.excerpt: To start, PCs earn enough XP to Level Up every 2-4 sessions. It takes more sessions to Level Up as PCs advance—the XP cost increases, and players roll 6- less often. A PC can Level Up only at home, which encourages players to return to Ston…

966.title: Slowing the rate
966.breadcrumb: Starting a session › Slowing the rate
966.excerpt: Ideally, PCs should Level Up only once between adventures. You want players to see each new move in play before they gain another. If your players often Level Up multiple times between adventures, consider some of the following. Reset XP to…

967.title: Higher-level play
967.excerpt: Past level 6, PCs become increasingly competent and harder to challenge. They have better stats, more ways of generating advantage, and lots of tools for overcoming challenges and mitigating dangers. They can start to feel invincible. To co…

968.title: Retiring PCs
968.excerpt: A PC might exit play because: - They die and pass through the Last Door - They retire after a lasting injury, as per Convalesce - Surplus runs short in winter , and the weariest PC's player has them die, leave, or retire - They cross the li…

969.title: Becoming a threat
969.excerpt: When a PC descends into villainy and monstrosity, they stop being a PC and become a threat under the GM's control. Write them up as both a threat and a monster , using their established moves, possessions, and abilities as inspiration. Don'…

970.title: Retirement
970.excerpt: If a PC retires from play but stays in the village or the region, then turn them into an NPC. By default, their portrayal falls to you. But if everyone agrees, their original player can portray them. Whoever portrays them, don't let them ov…

971.title: Epilogues
971.excerpt: When a PC retires or passes away, give them an epilogue: a scene or two to send them off and say goodbye. If the PC died, play out the funeral or wake. Establish the local traditions, if you haven't already. Show how NPCs react. Ask the sur…

972.title: New PCs
972.excerpt: You might need to introduce a new PC to an ongoing game when: - A new player joins your group - A PC dies, retires, or otherwise leaves play (see previous page), and the player creates a replacement PC If you're adding a player to an ongoin…

973.title: Creating the character
973.excerpt: Have the player pick one of the unused playbooks. They should also pick a background that reflects either a new arrival in Stonetop or a local NPC stepping up to PC status—each playbook has at least two such backgrounds. You don't want this…

974.title: Introductions
974.excerpt: Have the player stop at the Introductions part of their playbook. Decide together how you'll bring them into the game. There are two basic approaches: Play it out . Introduce the new PC in play. Frame a scene with them arriving in town, or…

975.title: Getting them involved
975.excerpt: Once they're in play, make sure the new PC matters. Prepare hooks that touch on their background, instincts, or unresolved questions. Work their worldbuilding elements into the game. Give them a little more spotlight, and allow them to find…

976.title: Conflict between PCs
976.excerpt: One of your players' agendas is to portray a compelling character , and such characters won't always agree. That's okay. Actually, that's great! Handled well, conflict between PCs can be high drama and some of the most memorable parts of th…

977.title: Intractable differences
977.excerpt: Over time, disagreements can grow into ongoing conflicts. The Judge won't stand for the Fox's deceit. The Fox can't forgive the Heavy for his husband's death. The Lightbearer frets over the state of the Seeker's soul. When you first sense s…

978.title: Open conflict
978.excerpt: When PCs directly oppose each other through violence, subterfuge, sabotage, or whatever—then slow down. A lot. First, do a temperature check. If things feel heated between the players, then call time out. Check in. Make sure everyone wants…

979.title: Descent into villainy
979.excerpt: Sometimes, a PC starts down a dark path. Maybe they keep using a major arcanum and racking up consequences. Maybe they are corrupted by the Things Below. Maybe they die but refuse to pass on, and linger as a Ghost , Revenant , or Thrall . M…

980.title: Regarding secrets
980.excerpt: When PCs come into conflict, it's natural that they will start lying to each other, or keeping secrets. Great! More drama! But don't let players keep secrets from each other. Don't accept notes they pass you. Don't have private chats or hud…

981.title: Time skips
981.excerpt: Stonetop often has you gloss over stretches of time. You might cover days, weeks, or months of travel or downtime with little more than a brief description and a few questions for the PCs. But sometimes, you want to zoom way out and jump fa…

982.title: Months, seasons, years
982.excerpt: If you're skipping a few months, seasons, or years, then write love letters to cover the gap. These can be fairly involved, including not only a roll and its results, but also invitations to narrate events and/or to involve the other player…

983.title: Up to a decade or two
983.breadcrumb: Retiring PCs › Up to a decade or two
983.excerpt: If you're skipping more than a few years, up to a decade or two, take a session off from actual play. Instead, discuss: - How long, exactly, are you skipping? - Who wants to continue playing their current PC? How might they change? - Who wa…

984.title: Generations
984.excerpt: If you jump ahead generations, then you're starting a whole new game. Hold a new first session . Discuss how the setting has changed in light of the game that just ended. Make and introduce new PCs, keeping those changes in mind. Ask questi…

985.title: Shake-ups
985.excerpt: The PCs all die, or leave Stonetop for good. The village is razed and its people scattered. A cataclysm is unleashed upon the world. Big changes like these can upend your game. Don't panic. Talk to the players about how they'd like to proce…

986.title: Option 1: End the game
986.breadcrumb: Retiring PCs › Option 1: End the game
986.excerpt: The right answer is often the simplest: call it quits. If the story feels complete, or the shake-up has stolen the game's momentum, then let the game end. Consider using the Denouement move if that kind of closure feels right. Take a break.…

987.title: Option 2: Pivot
987.excerpt: Keep playing, adjusting the game to reflect the shake-up. Skip forward an appropriate amount of time (see previous page). Make any mechanical changes you need. The specifics will vary case by case. Pivots like this can be hard. They make yo…

988.title: Ending your game
988.excerpt: All stories end. Maybe the story of your Stonetop burns bright and fast. Maybe it stretches on for years. Maybe it just… fizzles out. And that's fine. Not every story gets a good ending. Ideally, though, you'll see the end coming. Maybe rea…

989.title: Planning for the end
989.breadcrumb: Ending your game › Planning for the end
989.excerpt: When you see the end coming, you have an opportunity. An opportunity for climax, denouement, and a fitting farewell. A chance to give your shared story the final chapter it deserves. 1 Schedule a planning session Once you realize it's time…

990.title: 2 Prepare
990.excerpt: Ask your players to review the questions below, and give them some thought before the planning session. If they can't attend, ask them to send you their wish lists beforehand. Encourage everyone else to come with notes, or at least thoughts…

991.title: 3 Assemble the wishlist
991.breadcrumb: Ending your game › 3 Assemble the wishlist
991.excerpt: Once you've gathered for the planning session, refer to the questions from step 2, above. Print them out if playing in person, or post them somewhere everyone can see. The goal here is to establish each player's wishlist of things they'd li…

992.title: Driving toward resolution
992.excerpt: From now on, push play towards those must-have and important wishes. Work them into your prep. Keep the wish list handy as a reference during play, and get to the "nice to have" wishes as you can. Make use of techniques for increasing the p…

994.title: IMPORTANT

995.title: NICE TO HAVE
995.breadcrumb: Driving toward resolution › NICE TO HAVE
995.excerpt: - Tegwen gets her comeuppance (Blodwen, Rhianna) - Get back at Mutra (Vahid) - See Eira in retirement (Rhianna) - Morwenna's wedding (Caradoc) - Punch Brennan's face (Caradoc) moves that resolve situations, rather than escalating or drawing…

996.title: The final sessions
996.breadcrumb: Driving toward resolution › The final sessions
996.excerpt: Figure out when your last session will be, and how many sessions you have before then. As you prep for those final sessions, keep the wishlist forefront in your mind. Look at what you need to cover and how many sessions you have left. Use t…

997.title: The end
997.excerpt: When the final conflict ends and the dust starts to settle, take a breath. Then wrap up the current situation to whatever level of detail makes sense. Don't use any moves or mechanics. Just describe the scene and give the players space to s…

1033.excerpt: - Portray a compelling character - Engage with the fictional world - Play to find out what happens

1034.title: Player's Principles
1034.excerpt: - Begin and end with the fiction - Connect with the other PCs - Show us what's important to you - Have goals and pursue them - Be bold, take risks - Embrace difficulty, setback, and failure - Participate in worldbuilding - Build on what oth…

1035.title: When in doubt…
1035.excerpt: - Visualize the situation - Ask the GM for clarification - What do you want? What's your goal? - Consider your strengths and weaknesses - Look to others for ideas - Go with the obvious choice, the interesting choice, the meaningful choice..…

1036.title: Triggering moves

1047.title: Instinct

1051.excerpt: (granted) When you push the limits of your undead body , lose 1d4 HP and choose 1: - Perform a feat of inhuman strength - Act with uncanny speed and grace - Refuse to be moved, held back, or knocked off course

1054.title: Thrall
1054.excerpt: When you die but call on one of the Things Below by name and beseech them to intercede , they do. Death is averted, but you gain this insert and 1 Mark (see below).

1055.title: Your master
1055.excerpt: The Thing Below that you called upon? The one that plucked your soul from the Last Door and hides you from the Pale Hunter? It owns you now. Name your master here, along with any titles that you know: \ \ \

1056.excerpt: Ask the GM to choose 1, to represent your master's nature and will: - [ ] Stoke conflict, confusion, distrust - [ ] Erode hope/faith/honor/self-image - [ ] Hide/bury/smother things or ideas - [ ] Deprive others of what they need - [ ] Infli…

1057.title: Instinct
1057.excerpt: Lose the Instinct from your playbook, and replace it with one of these (your choice): - [ ] FASCINATION — To explore your powers, your master, your new existence. - [ ] RESISTANCE — To struggle against your master and cling to your humanity…

1058.title: Moves
1058.excerpt: You gain all of the following:

1059.title: FAVOR
1059.excerpt: (granted · Favor tracker: 3) Your Favor starts at 0 and can go no higher than 3. When you have 3 Favor and would gain another , reduce your Favor to 0 and choose 1: - Ask a question of your master and gain advantage on your next roll to act…

1060.title: URGES
1060.excerpt: (granted) When the GM compels you to act on your impulse , gain 1 Favor if you act as bidden. If you resist, roll +WIS: on a 10+ , your actions are your own; on a 7-9 , choose 1: - Struggle for control until something/one snaps you out of i…

1061.title: DARK SUCCOR
1061.excerpt: (granted) When you are dying or killed outright, your master intercedes on your behalf. You will recover, here and now or at a time and place of the GM's choosing. Then, roll +Favor: on a 10+ , choose 1; on a 7-9 , choose 2; on a 6- , all 3…

1062.title: UNHOLY VESSEL
1062.excerpt: (granted) When you would gain a Mark but there are none left to gain , your humanity is utterly lost. You become a threat in the GM's control. Make a new character.

1063.title: Thrall · Marks

1064.title: The Blessed
1064.excerpt: Danu, the Great Mother, provides. We need only learn her secrets: the names by which the trees call each other; the mark made with redberry juice to ward off impure spirits; the language of the wolves. A thousand such secrets Danu keeps, to…

1065.title: Background
1065.excerpt: Choose 1. - [ ] INITIATE Stonetop has long been home to a sacred order, keepers of the old ways and speakers for Danu. You are one such initiate, the most gifted in generations. You gain the Rites of the Land move. There are other initiates…

1072.title: BARKSKIN
1072.excerpt: When you are touching the earth , you have 2 armor. When you mark another with 1 Stock , they gain this benefit so long as the mark remains.

1073.title: BIG MAGIC
1073.excerpt: (may be taken twice) Each time you take this move, choose an additional remarkable trait for your sacred pouch and increase your max Stock by 2.

1074.title: DANU'S GRASP

1075.title: HEALER'S ARTS
1075.excerpt: When someone Recovers under your care , they recover (extra) HP equal to your WIS. If you also spend 1 Stock , they heal an extra 5 HP and their wounds/injuries are stabilized.

1076.title: HEED MY WORDS
1076.breadcrumb: The Blessed › Moves › HEED MY WORDS
1076.excerpt: When you Persuade by talking sense or warning against foolishness , you have advantage.

1077.title: IMPROVED STAT
1077.excerpt: (may be taken 3 times) Each time you take this move, increase one of your stats by 1 (to a max of +2).

1078.excerpt: When you approach a beast calmly and show no fear , it will not harm you (though it may threaten you and test your nerve). When you lay your hand gently upon a beast , it calms to your touch.

1079.title: LIGHTNING ROD
1079.excerpt: When you Defend while touching the earth , you can spend 1 Readiness to intercept a nearby magical attack and redirect it harmlessly into the ground.

1081.title: SPIRIT TONGUE
1081.excerpt: (starting move) You can speak with beasts and spirits. You can always ask the GM, "What spirits are active here?" and get an honest answer.

1082.title: BORROW POWER

1087.excerpt: (Requires level 2+ and the Blessed; may be taken twice) Each time you take this move, gain a Ranger move of your choice for which you qualify. You can't pick Improved Stat or Superior Stat.

1088.excerpt: (Requires level 6+ and Danu's Grasp) Danu's Grasp gains the area tag and can affect any creature. A mortal reduced to 0 HP is subdued or killed (your choice) rather than bound in stone.

1089.title: POTENT WORKINGS
1089.excerpt: (Requires level 6+ and Amulets & Talismans) When you craft a protective charm , you may spend 1 additional Stock to choose 1: - Name an additional type of harm - On a 10+, the charm retains its potency

1092.title: SUPERIOR STAT
1092.excerpt: (Requires level 6+) Increase one of your stats by +1 (to a max of +3).

1093.excerpt: (Requires level 6+ and Spirit Tongue) When you speak on behalf of Danu , natural beasts and spirits of the wild respect your authority. All but the most headstrong will do as you command, even against their instincts.

1108.excerpt: When you carry a light load and act with care , you move silently. When you hide in shadows or darkness , you remain unseen until you draw attention to yourself, move positions, or attack.

1109.excerpt: (Requires level 2+ and the Fox; may be taken 3 times) Each time you take this move, choose a move from the Heavy, Marshal, Ranger, or Seeker playbooks for which you otherwise qualify. (You can't take Improved Stat or Superior Stat.)

1110.excerpt: You can always ask the GM, "Is there an ambush or trap here?" If they say "yes," roll +INT: on a 10+ , ask the GM both of the questions below; on a 7-9 , ask 1; either way , gain advantage on your next roll to act on the answer(s). - What w…

1112.excerpt: (may be taken 3 times) Each time you take this move, increase one of your stats by 1 (to a max of +2).

1113.excerpt: When you interact with someone , you can ask their player if they find you attractive and get an honest answer (usually "yes"). When you Persuade by using your considerable charms as leverage , you have advantage.

1114.title: LAUGH AT DANGER

1114.excerpt: When you are about to roll +CON and you make a joke about the adversity you face , you can roll +CHA instead.

1115.title: LIGHT FINGERS
1115.excerpt: When you perform sleight of hand on an unwary mark , you succeed and no one's the wiser. If you're being watched, roll +DEX: on a 10+ , you succeed and no one's the wiser; on a 7-9 , you succeed OR no one's the wiser (your choice).

1116.title: PERCEPTIVE
1116.excerpt: When you Seek Insight , you may ask 1 additional question. Even on a 6-, you can ask 1 question (though you might not like how you learn the answer).

1117.title: RAPIER WIT

1118.title: SKILL AT ARMS
1118.breadcrumb: The Fox › Moves › SKILL AT ARMS
1118.excerpt: When you wield a weapon with speed and grace , roll +DEX to Clash (instead of +STR).

1119.excerpt: (Requires Skill at Arms) When you Defend with a weapon that you can wield quickly , you can spend 1 Readiness to both halve an attack's effects/damage and strike back at the attacker (deal your damage with disadvantage), instead of spending…

1120.excerpt: When you use words to avoid suspicion or trouble , roll +CHA: on a 10+ , hold 3 Nerve; on a 7-9 , hold 1 Nerve. You may spend Nerve, 1-for-1, to: - Move about or maneuver unchallenged - Withstand direct scrutiny or questioning - Direct susp…

1122.title: BATTLE DANCER
1122.excerpt: (Requires level 6+ and Skill at Arms) When you roll +DEX to Clash , on a 12+ you deal your damage, avoid your enemy's attack, and impress/embarrass/overawe your foes.

1123.title: CHEAP SHOT
1123.excerpt: (Requires level 6+ and Ambush) When you Ambush with a hand weapon , you have advantage on your damage roll.

1124.title: EYE ON THE DOOR
1124.breadcrumb: The Fox › Moves › EYE ON THE DOOR
1124.excerpt: (Requires level 6+) When you and your allies need to get out of here , name your escape route and roll +INT: on a 10+ , you're gone; on a 7-9 , you can stay or go, but if you go, it costs you—the GM will tell you what (or who) you leave beh…

1125.title: PANTS ON FIRE
1125.breadcrumb: The Fox › Moves › PANTS ON FIRE
1125.excerpt: (Requires level 6+) When you Defy Danger, Persuade, or Interfere by being deceitful , you have advantage. When another move (like Seek Insight) allows a player to ask you a question, you can opt not to answer.

1126.title: SECOND INTENT
1126.excerpt: (Requires level 6+, Parry & Riposte, and Ambush) When you Defend and spend 1 Readiness to Parry & Riposte , also pick 1 option from the Ambush list.

1127.title: SLIPPERY

1128.title: SUPERIOR STAT

1142.title: BERSERKER
1142.breadcrumb: The Heavy › Moves › BATTLE JOY › BERSERKER
1142.excerpt: (Requires Battle Joy) While in your Battle Joy , add the area tag to your melee attacks, lashing out at anyone nearby (friend and foe alike). Roll damage separately for each target.

1143.title: CARVED OUT OF WOOD
1143.breadcrumb: The Heavy › Moves › CARVED OUT OF WOOD

1144.title: DANGEROUS
1144.excerpt: (starting move; requires the Heavy) When you deal your damage , you have advantage.

1145.title: FORMIDABLE
1145.excerpt: When you wade into battle , you can choose to roll +CHA: on a 10+ , both; on a 7-9 , pick 1: - Lesser foes will quail, hesitate, or flee before you. - Doughty foes will focus on you, seeing you as the greatest threat. On a 6- , pick 1 but a…

1146.title: FROSTY
1146.excerpt: When you Defy Danger by keeping calm and carrying on , on a 10+ you can also ask the GM a question that you could ask when Seeking Insight. You have advantage on your next move to act on the answer.

1147.title: GUARDIAN
1147.excerpt: When you Defend , hold 1 extra Readiness. Even on a 6-, hold 1 Readiness (plus whatever the GM says).

1148.title: IMPROVED STAT
1148.excerpt: (may be taken 3 times) Each time you take this move, increase one of your stats by 1 (to a max of +2).

1149.title: INTIMIDATING
1149.excerpt: When you Persuade using violence or threats , you have advantage.

1150.title: HARD TO KILL
1150.breadcrumb: The Heavy › Moves › HARD TO KILL
1150.excerpt: (starting move) When you are at Death's Door, you can roll +CON or +nothing (your choice). On a 7-9 , you can mark a debility of your choice to regain 1 HP.

1151.title: UNSTOPPABLE
1151.breadcrumb: The Heavy › Moves › HARD TO KILL › UNSTOPPABLE
1151.excerpt: (Requires Hard to Kill; tracker: 5) When you are reduced to 0 HP in battle , you can keep fighting. Each time you take damage while at 0 HP, mark 1. If you would regain HP while fighting, clear one mark instead. When you stop fighting , rol…

1152.title: MUSCLEBOUND
1152.excerpt: (Requires Strength +2 or higher) When you make a hand-to-hand or thrown attack , it's forceful and messy . If it would already be forceful and/or messy , it's even more so.

1153.title: PAYBACK
1153.excerpt: When you deal damage to a foe that has harmed you or one of your allies , deal +1d4 damage.

1154.title: RELENTLESS
1154.excerpt: When you Clash and your foe survives , you gain advantage the next time you Clash with them.

1155.title: SEASONED WARRIOR
1155.excerpt: (Requires level 2+ and the Heavy; may be taken 3 times) Take a move from the Fox, Marshal, Ranger, or Seeker playbooks, for which you otherwise qualify. You can pick from a different playbook each time. (You can't pick Improved Stat or Supe…

1156.title: SITUATIONAL AWARENESS
1156.excerpt: When you Seek Insight , add the following to the list of questions you can ask: - Who or what here is the biggest threat? - What is my enemy's true position? - What here can I use as a weapon? When a fight breaks out , ask the GM 1 question…

1157.title: UNCANNY REFLEXES
1157.excerpt: When you are unarmored and carrying a normal or light load , you impose disadvantage on any damage you take that you could dodge or roll with.

1158.title: UNFETTERED
1158.excerpt: When you are subject to physical or mental restraint , you may mark a debility to immediately break free of that restraint.

1159.title: TERROR ON THE FIELD
1159.breadcrumb: The Heavy › Moves › TERROR ON THE FIELD
1159.excerpt: When you reduce a foe to 0 HP , describe how you take them out. If you fell them in a particularly brutal or impressive manner, their allies are impressed, dismayed, or frightened and respond accordingly.

1160.title: BRINGER OF RUIN
1160.breadcrumb: The Heavy › Moves › BRINGER OF RUIN
1160.excerpt: (Requires level 6+) When you roll a 12+ to Clash and your foe survives , name something they possess (like their sword, their position, a limb, their dignity, etc.), but nothing that would kill them outright. Whatever you name, it is broken…

1161.title: CUT FROM GRANITE
1161.breadcrumb: The Heavy › Moves › CUT FROM GRANITE
1161.excerpt: (Requires level 6+ and Carved Out of Wood) Gain +1 armor (stacks with other sources) and increase your max HP by another 2 (+6 HP total).

1162.title: MIGHTY THEWS
1162.excerpt: (Requires level 6+ and Musclebound) When you perform a feat of extraordinary strength (bursting chains, smashing through a wall, heaving a boulder, etc.), you do it (OH YEAH!) but pick 1: - It takes a while - You cause unwanted damage or ha…

1163.title: NEMESIS
1163.excerpt: (Requires level 6+ and Relentless) When you Clash and your foe survives , all of your future attacks against them do +1d6 damage.

1164.title: STEADFAST GUARDIAN
1164.excerpt: (Requires level 6+ and Guardian) While you hold Readiness (from Defend) , you can always suffer the damage/effects of an attack instead of your ward; no need to spend Readiness, you can just do it.

1165.title: STONE COLD
1165.excerpt: (Requires level 6+ and Frosty) When you Defy Danger (or Struggle as One) by keeping calm and carrying on , treat a 6- as a 7-9.

1166.title: SUPERIOR STAT
1166.excerpt: (Requires level 6+) Increase one of your stats by +1 (to a max of +3).

1179.excerpt: When you speak the truth with conviction and candor , none can doubt you. They might deny what you say, but in their hearts they recognize the truth.

1180.title: BREAK BREAD
1180.excerpt: When you share a proper meal with someone and each of you eats their fill , each of you recovers 1d8 (extra) HP.

1181.title: BULWARK
1181.excerpt: When you Defend , you can spend 1 Readiness to stand fast, holding your position despite what befalls you.

1182.title: CENSURE

1183.title: CASTIGATE
1183.excerpt: (Requires level 2+, Censure) When you Censure someone , your voice deals 1d4 damage to them ( near , loud , ignores armor).

1195.excerpt: When you Seek Insight , you can always ask "What here is hidden by illusion or magic?" for free, even on a 6-.

1197.excerpt: (Requires level 6+; replaces Bulwark) When you hold Readiness (from Defend), you cannot be forced from your position. Also, you can spend 1 Readiness to completely ignore the effects/damage of an attack that you suffer.

1198.title: ARMISTICE
1198.excerpt: (Requires level 6+ and Bear Witness) When you approach an enemy to negotiate in good faith , they will at least hear you out. Even the most debased and savage foe will delay violence until you've had your say.

1199.title: CONDEMN

1394.excerpt: You start with Anger is a Gift, Potential for Greatness, and 2 other moves of your choice.

1395.title: ANGER IS A GIFT
1395.breadcrumb: The Would-be Hero › Moves › ANGER IS A GIFT
1395.excerpt: (starting move · Resolve tracker: 2) When you burn with righteous anger (see Fear & Anger on back of playbook), hold 2 Resolve. You can spend your Resolve 1-for-1 to: - Set aside fear and doubt to do what must be done - Act suddenly, catchi…

1396.title: SPEAK TRUTH TO POWER
1396.breadcrumb: The Would-be Hero › Moves › ANGER IS A GIFT › SPEAK TRUTH TO POWER
1396.excerpt: (Requires Anger is a Gift) When you demand that someone does what is clearly good and right , you have advantage to Persuade. If they refuse, gain +1 Resolve.

1397.title: BETTER PART OF VALOR
1397.breadcrumb: The Would-be Hero › Moves › BETTER PART OF VALOR
1397.excerpt: When you are outnumbered or facing a foe bigger than you , you have advantage to hide from, escape from, or sneak past them.

1398.title: I GET KNOCKED DOWN
1398.breadcrumb: The Would-be Hero › Moves › I GET KNOCKED DOWN
1398.excerpt: When you take damage despite your best efforts to avoid it , you can choose to halve the damage but pick 1 of the following: - You lose something (footing, grip, etc.) - Something on your person breaks - You're out of it for a moment Whatev…

1399.title: BUT I GET UP AGAIN
1399.breadcrumb: The Would-be Hero › Moves › I GET KNOCKED DOWN › BUT I GET UP AGAIN
1399.excerpt: (Requires I Get Knocked Down) When you use I Get Knocked Down , you have advantage on your next roll against whatever dealt the damage and your next blow against them does +1d4 damage.

1400.title: IMPROVED STAT
1400.breadcrumb: The Would-be Hero › Moves › IMPROVED STAT
1400.excerpt: (may be taken 3 times) Each time you take this move, increase one of your stats by 1 (to a max of +2).

1401.title: IN OVER YOUR HEAD
1401.breadcrumb: The Would-be Hero › Moves › IN OVER YOUR HEAD
1401.excerpt: When another PC rescues you from danger , mark XP.

1402.title: IRON WILL
1402.breadcrumb: The Would-be Hero › Moves › IRON WILL
1402.excerpt: When you are subject to mind control or magic that affects your feelings , you can take 1d4 damage (ignoring armor) to disregard its influence.

1403.title: INQUIRING MINDS
1403.breadcrumb: The Would-be Hero › Moves › INQUIRING MINDS
1403.excerpt: When you seek out and receive honest advice , gain advantage on your next roll to follow that advice.

1404.title: NEVER GONNA KEEP ME DOWN
1404.breadcrumb: The Would-be Hero › Moves › NEVER GONNA KEEP ME DOWN
1404.excerpt: (per-session tracker: 1) When you have 5 or fewer current HP , you impose disadvantage on any damage you take. Once per session, when you are at Death's Door, don't roll. You get a 10+.

1405.title: POTENTIAL FOR GREATNESS
1405.breadcrumb: The Would-be Hero › Moves › POTENTIAL FOR GREATNESS
1405.excerpt: (starting move · Requires the Would-be Hero) Once per level, when you roll a stat and get a 10+ , mark one of the following (note the level during which you marked it). You don't have to mark them in order. - [ ] Increase the stat you rolle…

1406.excerpt: Pick 2. - [ ] A heap of expectations : of little use - [ ] A good dog , follower ( retriever or herder , keen-nosed, clever); HP 6; Damage d6 ( hand , grabby ); Instinct to play; Cost: affection. - [ ] Husbandry tools : brushes, muzzles, co…

1407.title: Advanced moves
1407.excerpt: The first time you use any move marked with an asterisk (\ ), cross off "Would-be" on the front page.

1408.title: RESOURCEFUL
1408.breadcrumb: The Would-be Hero › Advanced moves › RESOURCEFUL
1408.excerpt: When you Defy Danger and roll a 6- , ask the GM a question from Seek Insight after they describe what happens. Gain advantage on your next roll to act on the answer.

1409.title: SOMETHING TO REMEMBER ME BY
1409.breadcrumb: The Would-be Hero › Advanced moves › SOMETHING TO REMEMBER ME BY
1409.excerpt: When you spend Readiness (from Defend) to strike back at an attacker , you deal +1d4 damage and scar, mark, or diminish them in some way (the GM will say how, or ask you to).

1410.title: TOUGH LOVE
1410.breadcrumb: The Would-be Hero › Advanced moves › TOUGH LOVE
1410.excerpt: When you honestly think another PC is in the wrong and call them on it , they have disadvantage on any rolls against you until you two work it out.

1411.title: UNDERESTIMATED
1411.breadcrumb: The Would-be Hero › Advanced moves › UNDERESTIMATED
1411.excerpt: As long as you avoid overt hostility , no enemy will consider you a threat. When you first make your move against an enemy who underestimates you , you have advantage.

1412.title: UP WITH PEOPLE
1412.breadcrumb: The Would-be Hero › Advanced moves › UP WITH PEOPLE
1412.excerpt: (Rapport trackers: 2 / 1) When you converse with someone (PC or NPC) you can hold 2 Rapport with them. If you do, they hold 1 Rapport with you. During the conversation, either of you can spend 1 Rapport to ask the other player one of the fo…

1413.title: VERSATILE
1413.breadcrumb: The Would-be Hero › Advanced moves › VERSATILE
1413.excerpt: (Requires level 2+, the Would-be Hero · may be taken 4 times) Choose a move from any other playbook, as long as you meet its requirements. You can pick from a different playbook each time. You can't take Improved Stat or Superior Stat.

1414.title: A FORCE TO BE RECKONED WITH\
1414.breadcrumb: The Would-be Hero › Advanced moves › A FORCE TO BE RECKONED WITH\
1414.excerpt: (Requires level 6+; replaces Underestimated) Any intelligent creature who looks you in the eye or hears the steel in your voice instinctively knows that you are a force to be reckoned with, and treats you appropriately. When you Defy Danger…

1415.title: BIG DAMN HERO\
1415.breadcrumb: The Would-be Hero › Advanced moves › BIG DAMN HERO\
1415.excerpt: (Requires level 6+; replaces In Over Your Head) When you first leap into danger to protect someone , don't roll to Defend. Instead, treat it as though you rolled a 10+. When you Defend , you can spend 1 Readiness to lock eyes with an attack…

1416.title: SUPERIOR STAT
1416.breadcrumb: The Would-be Hero › Advanced moves › SUPERIOR STAT
1416.excerpt: (Requires all 6 marks in Potential for Greatness) Increase one of your stats by +1 (to a max of +3).

1417.title: UNDAUNTED\
1417.breadcrumb: The Would-be Hero › Advanced moves › UNDAUNTED\
1417.excerpt: (Requires level 6+; replaces Better Part of Valor) When you are outnumbered or facing a foe bigger than you , you get +1 armor and deal +1d6 damage.

1418.title: VOICE OF EXPERIENCE\
1418.breadcrumb: The Would-be Hero › Advanced moves › VOICE OF EXPERIENCE\
1418.excerpt: (Requires level 6+; replaces Inquiring Minds) When another PC comes to you for advice and you tell them what you think is best , they have advantage on their first roll to follow your advice. When you Seek Insight , you can always ask, "Wha…
